Missing quality signals: No DOI, journal, or conference venue provided., No affiliation metadata provided., No citation count or h-index information provided., No code repository or software package mentioned., No public data availability statement mentioned., No ablation, sensitivity, or robustness details visible in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1, published 2026-06-25.; Single listed author: Hengshi Yu.; Abstract states consistency if either propensity score or outcome regression model is correctly specified.; Abstract compares DR-QIF with DR-GEE and reports up to 3.5% efficiency gain at N=120 and T=8.

TL;DR: This paper proposes a doubly robust quadratic inference function estimator for average treatment effects in cluster randomized trials with covariate imbalance and correlated longitudinal outcomes.

Problem: Existing QIF methods for marginal regression with correlated data do not address confounding from covariate imbalance between treatment arms, which matters in observational CRTs and CRT analyses with prognostic covariate adjustment.

Method: The paper combines doubly robust pseudo-outcomes, built from propensity score and outcome regression models, with QIF extended score equations to estimate average treatment effects under correlated CRT data.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element is a DR-QIF estimator that retains consistency if either the propensity score model or outcome regression model is correctly specified, while targeting efficiency gains over DR-GEE when the working correlation structure is misspecified.

Main result: From the abstract, DR-QIF is analytically shown to be more efficient than DR-GEE under misspecified working correlation; in cross-sectional CRTs the two are algebraically identical, while in longitudinal CRTs with strong temporal correlation the reported efficiency gain reaches 3.5% at N=120 and T=8.

Limitation: The abstract reports Monte Carlo simulation and one WASH Benefits Kenya application, but does not mention code, public implementation, robustness checks beyond the described simulations, or validation across multiple empirical datasets.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causal inference' because it studies doubly robust average treatment effect estimation in clustered randomized trial settings with confounding/covariate imbalance.

Tags: causal inference, doubly robust estimation, quadratic inference functions, cluster randomized trials, correlated data, marginal regression, Monte Carlo simulation

Figure note: A useful image would be a pipeline diagram showing how propensity score and outcome regression models form doubly robust pseudo-outcomes, which are then passed into QIF extended score equations for clustered longitudinal treatment-effect estimation.

TL;DR: The paper argues that DPO-aligned LLM trading agents develop highly correlated forecasting errors, undermining the independence assumption behind LLM-based prediction markets.

Problem: It studies whether prediction markets still aggregate independent signals when the participants are LLM agents, especially agents fine-tuned with Direct Preference Optimization.

Method: The abstract describes controlled comparisons of DPO-tuned agents against identical-SFT controls at 8B and 70B scales, measures pairwise error correlation and effective independent forecaster count, tests scaling from 5 to 40 agents, and evaluates mitigation strategies such as cross-model diversity.

Key contribution: The novel claim is that preference optimization itself causally drives correlated errors and market monoculture, rather than the issue being solved by adding more same-family LLM agents.

Main result: DPO agents show pairwise error correlation of rho=0.70, reducing 10 agents to about 1.4 effective independent forecasters; performance of a 10-agent market is reported as 67.6%, below a single standalone agent at 70.2%, and cross-model diversity reduces rho from 0.68 to 0.40.

Limitation: The abstract does not identify datasets, market-question selection, code availability, full baselines, statistical uncertainty, affiliations, or external validation beyond the stated lab and scale replications.

Relevance: The matched query is causality: the paper explicitly claims two controlled ablations isolate preference optimization as the causal driver of correlated LLM-agent errors in prediction markets.

item.title: Perception, Verdict, and Evolution: Hindsight-Driven Self-Refining Forensics Agent for AI-Generated Image Detection
item.description:
Priority: 45/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 16, Paper 35, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index data, DOI or venue acceptance, open-source code, repository quality, public release details for generated reasoning traces

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper in cs.CV and cs.AI, 10 pages, published 2026-06-25.; Reports 82.18% accuracy on Chameleon, +16.41 percentage points over AIDE.; Reports 93.3% mean accuracy on AIGCDetect-Benchmark across 16 generators.; Claims external evaluation against GPT-5 and GPT-5-mini for consistency and causal grounding.

TL;DR: ForeAgent is an agentic multimodal forensics framework that detects AI-generated images by combining multi-view forensic cues with MLLM-based verdict reasoning and iterative hindsight-driven self-refinement.

Problem: The paper studies AI-generated image and deepfake detection under rapidly improving generative models, where existing detectors may miss fine-grained forensic artifacts and depend on costly, static synthetic supervision from frontier models.

Method: It proposes ForeAgent, using a Perception-Verdict architecture that aggregates semantic, spatial, and frequency-domain cues, then uses an MLLM verdict module to fuse them into a reasoning-backed decision. It further introduces a Hindsight-Driven Self-Refining strategy with Sampling-Reflection-Evolution: inference rollouts are checked against labels, failed or weak reasoning traces are regenerated, filtered by a dual-expert quality gate, and used for continual fine-tuning.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element appears to be the combination of multi-view forensic perception, MLLM verdict reasoning, and iterative self-curation/self-evolution of reasoning traces for AI-image forensics, rather than relying only on static detector training or fixed synthetic supervision.

Main result: From the abstract, ForeAgent reports state-of-the-art performance on Chameleon with 82.18% accuracy, a +16.41 percentage point gain over AIDE, and 93.3% mean accuracy on AIGCDetect-Benchmark across 16 generators; external evaluation claims more consistent and causally grounded reasoning than GPT-5 and GPT-5-mini.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide code availability, data release, ablation details, robustness tests, statistical significance, model size/cost, or enough information to verify whether the causal-grounding claim is rigorously measured.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' because it explicitly claims more causally grounded reasoning for AI-generated image detection, and its agentic reflection/evolution mechanism may be relevant to researchers tracking causal reasoning in multimodal detection systems.

Tags: AI-generated image detection, deepfake detection, MLLM, agentic framework, self-refinement, forensics, causal reasoning

item.title: A Unified Three-Stage Weighting Framework for Causal Inference and Mediation Analysis under Case-Control Sampling
item.description:
Priority: 54/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 36, Paper 45, Empirical 70, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: author affiliations or prior topic record, citation count or h-index, DOI, journal, or conference venue, open-source code, public implementation details beyond abstract, dataset access details for the application

Score evidence: arXiv v1 posted 2026-06-25 in stat.ME.; Abstract states a unified three-stage weighting framework for causal inference and mediation analysis from case-control studies.; Method combines density-ratio learning, label-shift correction, prevalence-based design weights, and marginal structural modeling.; Empirical evidence mentioned includes simulation studies and an NHANES application.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a three-stage weighting framework to estimate causal and mediation effects from case-control studies despite retrospective, outcome-dependent sampling.

Problem: Case-control sampling improves efficiency for rare outcomes but distorts the population outcome distribution, making standard causal inference and causal mediation analyses biased when they ignore the sampling design.

Method: The proposed 3S-weighting framework first estimates population outcome prevalence using density-ratio learning, label-shift correction, and external covariate information; then uses prevalence-based design weights to reconstruct the target population; and finally applies stabilized causal and mediation weights in marginal structural models to estimate total and pathway-specific effects.

Key contribution: The novelty is a unified weighting pipeline that links prevalence recovery, case-control design correction, and marginal structural causal/mediation weighting, including decomposition into pure direct, pure indirect, and interaction effects.

Main result: The abstract reports simulations where conventional analyses that ignore retrospective sampling show substantial bias, while the proposed method consistently recovers target population causal parameters across sampling scenarios; an NHANES application illustrates implementation.

Limitation: The abstract does not report numerical effect sizes, comparison against multiple causal-inference baselines, ablations, robustness checks, code availability, or detailed assumptions required for density-ratio/label-shift correction.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the 'causal inference' feed because it directly studies causal effect and mediation estimation under a nonstandard but common epidemiologic sampling design.

Tags: causal inference, causal mediation, case-control studies, weighting, marginal structural models, density-ratio learning, label shift, epidemiology

Figure note: A pipeline figure showing the three stages, from prevalence estimation through target-population reconstruction to causal/mediation weighting, would be useful; the metadata says the paper has 3 figures but provides no direct image URL.

TL;DR: Nemotron-TwoTower proposes a two-tower diffusion language model that separates autoregressive context encoding from block-wise diffusion denoising, aiming to improve generation throughput while preserving most AR baseline quality.

Problem: The paper studies a limitation of diffusion language models: using a single network for both causal context representation and iterative denoising may constrain capacity and reduce effectiveness for both roles.

Method: It proposes TwoTower, a block-wise autoregressive diffusion framework with a frozen autoregressive context tower for clean-token causal processing and a trainable diffusion denoiser tower with bidirectional block attention, connected through cross-attention to the context.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element described in the abstract is the architectural decoupling of pretrained AR context processing from diffusion denoising, implemented on top of Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B rather than training one shared network to do both jobs.

Main result: According to the abstract, Nemotron-TwoTower is trained on about 2.1T tokens, retains 98.7% of the autoregressive baseline's quality, and achieves 2.42x higher wall-clock generation throughput.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify benchmark names, metric definitions, ablations, robustness tests, statistical uncertainty, or detailed comparisons against other diffusion language models.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' because it describes causal processing in the autoregressive context tower, but it appears to be about causal attention/generation order in language modeling rather than causality or causal inference as a scientific topic.

item.title: Epiphany-Aware KV Cache Eviction Without the Attention Matrix
item.description:
Priority: 41/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 14, Paper 35, Empirical 70, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 65

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations not supplied., Citation count and h-index not supplied., No DOI, venue, or acceptance signal supplied., No code repository or data release mentioned., No ablations, robustness tests, statistical uncertainty, or limitation discussion visible in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 preprint marked 'in review'; no DOI or journal reference provided.; Method claims no training, classifier, custom kernel, or attention matrix.; Reports MATH-500, AIME-2024, baseline comparisons against ThinKV and H2O, and up to 2.8x speed.; Matched query is 'causality', but the abstract only shows a 'causal rolling z-score' mechanism.

TL;DR: EpiKV proposes a training-free KV cache eviction method for long reasoning traces that scores token importance from representation changes instead of attention weights, enabling FlashAttention-compatible inference with reported accuracy and speed gains under fixed cache budgets.

Problem: The paper studies KV cache growth as a deployment bottleneck for reasoning models that generate very long chains of thought, arguing that attention-weight-based eviction is noisy and incompatible with fused attention kernels because it requires materializing attention matrices.

Method: It introduces an epiphany score based on changes in the model's internal representation observed during the forward pass, then uses this score for cache eviction without training, classifiers, attention-matrix materialization, custom kernels, or changes to FlashAttention stacks. The abstract also mentions layer-sign handling and a causal rolling z-score to remove positional trends, but the exact algorithmic details are only partially specified.

Key contribution: The main novelty is replacing attention-weight importance proxies with a forward-pass representation-change metric for KV eviction, allowing production-style fused-kernel inference while reportedly scaling feasible context length up to 16x beyond attention-based scoring methods.

Main result: At a 4096-token cache, EpiKV reports 72% on MATH-500, comparable to ThinKV at 71% and above H2O at 67%; a lag-normalized variant reports 37% on AIME-2024 at 8192 tokens versus 33% for the best compared baseline, with up to 2.8x speedup.

Limitation: The abstract does not mention code release, public implementation details, ablations, robustness tests, statistical uncertainty, broader datasets beyond MATH-500 and AIME-2024, or independent validation. The causality relevance appears weak: the match likely comes from 'causal rolling z-score' rather than causal inference or causal modeling.

Relevance: Relevant mainly if the researcher tracks long-context LLM inference, KV cache compression, or reasoning-model deployment. For the configured query 'causality', relevance is low because the paper appears to use 'causal' in the temporal/online-normalization sense, not as a causality research contribution.

Tags: KV-cache, LLM-inference, long-context, reasoning-models, FlashAttention, cache-eviction, weak-causality-match

Figure note: A useful figure would show the EpiKV inference pipeline: representation-change scoring during the forward pass, causal rolling z-score normalization, token ranking, and KV eviction inside a FlashAttention-compatible stack.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-25.; Comment states 20 pages, 7 figures, 17 tables, and 43rd ICML Workshop on Foundation Models for Structured Data.; Abstract says TabPFN-CFM predicts both causal structure and outcomes from observational data.; Abstract says it supports all three levels of Pearl's Causal Hierarchy.

TL;DR: TabPFN-CFM is a causal foundation model for observational tabular data that jointly predicts causal structure and outcomes and supports queries across Pearl's causal hierarchy.

Problem: The paper studies how to build a single model that can address multiple causal tasks from observational data, including causal structure prediction, outcome prediction, and causal queries at association, intervention, and counterfactual levels.

Method: It proposes TabPFN-CFM, trained on synthetic datasets, that predicts causal graphs and outcomes from observational data and can incorporate known graph structure when available.

Key contribution: The novelty claimed in the abstract is a unified causal foundation model that handles both structure and outcome prediction and supports all three levels of Pearl's Causal Hierarchy, rather than focusing on only graph discovery or only treatment/outcome estimation.

Main result: The abstract reports generalization from synthetic training data to real datasets and improved performance over both structural and outcome prediction baselines, but does not provide quantitative metrics.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify datasets, baseline names, effect sizes, ablations, robustness checks, code availability, or failure modes.

Relevance: This is highly relevant to the matched query 'causality' because it directly targets causal structure learning, causal outcome prediction, and causal queries in a foundation-model framework.

Tags: causality, causal discovery, foundation models, tabular data, Pearl causal hierarchy, observational data, cs.LG

Figure note: A useful image would be the model or pipeline figure showing how TabPFN-CFM takes observational data and optional graph information to produce structure, outcome, and causal-hierarchy query predictions.

item.title: ProvenAI: Provenance-Native Traces of Evidence in Generated Answers
item.description:
Priority: 53/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 40, Paper 45, Empirical 68, Reproducibility 45, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: No DOI, journal, or conference venue listed., No affiliations, citation counts, or h-index metadata supplied for authors., No code repository or implementation-release signal in the metadata/abstract., No explicit baselines, statistical tests, robustness checks, or cross-dataset evaluation reported in the abstract., No detailed model configuration or retrieval/generation implementation details visible from the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 published 2026-06-24 in cs.CL, cs.AI, cs.CR, and cs.IR.; Evaluates 7,405 HotpotQA distractor validation examples from a 509,300-passage corpus.; Reports 53.53% answer accuracy and 71.55% mean citation-fidelity score.; Uses leave-one-resource-out intervention to estimate per-document influence.

TL;DR: ProvenAI proposes a provenance-focused evaluation framework for RAG-style multi-hop QA that separates answer correctness, citation fidelity, and causal document influence.

Problem: The paper studies whether citations shown beside generated answers actually reflect the evidence that shaped the model output, especially in retrieval-augmented multi-hop QA.

Method: It introduces ProvenAI, a seven-stage pipeline for HotpotQA distractor QA covering data normalization, retrieval indexing, citation-aware generation, attribution auditing, leave-one-resource-out influence estimation, batch evaluation, and interactive inspection.

Key contribution: The main novelty is treating retrieved, cited, and behaviorally influential evidence as distinct transparency layers, with per-document influence measured through interventions rather than inferred from citations alone.

Main result: On 7,405 HotpotQA validation examples from a 509,300-passage corpus, the system reports 53.53% answer accuracy and 71.55% mean citation-fidelity score; a worked example shows a citation-influence gap where cited evidence can have weak influence while uncited sources shift the output.

Limitation: The abstract does not report baselines, ablations beyond the leave-one-resource-out mechanism, statistical uncertainty, robustness checks, model details, code availability, or validation beyond HotpotQA distractor.

Relevance: The paper matches the causality-focused feed because it frames RAG transparency through causal-style intervention and cites causal-mediation analysis as part of its theoretical grounding.

Tags: retrieval-augmented generation, provenance, citation fidelity, causal influence, multi-hop QA, HotpotQA, attribution auditing

Figure note: A useful figure would show the ProvenAI seven-stage pipeline and the three transparency layers linking retrieved documents, cited sources, and leave-one-resource-out influence scores.

item.title: Staying VIGILant: Mitigating Visual Laziness via Counterfactual Visual Alignment in MLLMs
item.description:
Priority: 51/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 8, Paper 70, Empirical 74, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index information, DOI, journal reference, specific benchmark names, numerical results

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper dated 2026-06-24.; Comment field says ECCV 2026.; Abstract proposes VIGIL as an RL post-training framework for MLLM hallucination mitigation.; Abstract explicitly uses counterfactual blind state masking and mutual information between visual input and response.

TL;DR: VIGIL is an RL post-training framework for multimodal LLMs that uses counterfactual visual masking and a mutual-information-style grounding constraint to reduce visually contradicted hallucinations.

Problem: The paper studies hallucinations in multimodal large language models, especially cases where models internally encode relevant visual evidence but over-rely on language priors during response generation, a phenomenon the abstract calls visual laziness.

Method: It proposes Visual Information Gain In aLignment (VIGIL), an RL post-training framework that penalizes blind confidence by comparing normal generation against a counterfactual blind state created by masking textual-visual attention, with the goal of increasing mutual information between visual input and generated response.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is shifting alignment from outcome-level text reward fitting toward causal visual grounding through a geometric constraint and counterfactual visual masking, rather than relying mainly on preference optimization over textual outputs.

Main result: The abstract claims VIGIL consistently outperforms recent alignment methods on hallucination and reasoning benchmarks, preserves text-only capabilities, matches full-data state-of-the-art performance with 25% of preference data, and shows emergent spatial grounding without bounding-box supervision.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide benchmark names, numerical gains, ablation details, code availability, data availability, robustness checks, or enough implementation detail to judge reproducibility.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the causality query because it frames MLLM hallucination mitigation through counterfactual intervention and causal visual grounding rather than only reward optimization.

Tags: MLLM, visual hallucination, counterfactual alignment, reinforcement learning, visual grounding, causality, mutual information

Figure note: A useful image would be the VIGIL framework diagram showing normal visual-text generation, the counterfactual blind masked-attention state, and the blind-confidence penalty/mutual-information constraint.

item.title: Variance Deltas for Visualizing and Explaining Posterior Uncertainty
item.description:
Priority: 44/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 26, Paper 38, Empirical 48, Reproducibility 45, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: Author affiliation not supplied., Citation count and h-index not supplied., No DOI, journal, or conference signal supplied., No code repository URL supplied, despite software-package claim., No dataset availability details supplied., No baselines, ablations, statistical robustness checks, or user-study evidence visible in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-24 in stat.ME.; Abstract proposes an interactive visualization system called variance deltas.; System is described as a software package, but no repository URL is supplied in the metadata.; Demonstrations include a simulated causal inference treatment-effect problem and a real polling forecasting model.

TL;DR: The paper introduces “variance deltas,” an interactive visualization system for explaining which unobserved Bayesian model quantities would most reduce posterior uncertainty about a target estimand.

Problem: In observational settings, especially causal inference and forecasting, researchers often cannot guarantee that available data estimate quantities of interest precisely; the paper studies how to identify what missing or unobserved information would meaningfully reduce posterior uncertainty.

Method: The proposed system takes a Bayesian posterior distribution and organizes candidate subsets of unobserved model quantities into a tree according to how well each subset explains uncertainty in a quantity of interest. It automates candidate subset construction from minimal user input and supports interactive splitting and combining of subsets.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element, based on the abstract, is framing missing-information discovery as an interactive posterior-uncertainty explanation task, using tree-structured “variance deltas” rather than only reporting posterior intervals, sensitivity checks, or scalar uncertainty decompositions.

Main result: The abstract reports demonstrations on two cases: a simulated causal inference problem for a treatment effect parameter and a real polling forecasting model with many bias sources. It claims the system discovers nonobvious explanations of uncertainty, but gives no quantitative performance result in the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not report quantitative metrics, baselines against existing uncertainty-explanation tools, ablations, user-study evidence, robustness checks, or a code/data URL, so empirical strength and reproducibility cannot be fully assessed from the supplied metadata.

Relevance: This is relevant to the matched query “causal inference” because one demonstration targets uncertainty in a treatment effect parameter, and the method could help diagnose what missing information drives posterior uncertainty in observational causal analyses.

Tags: Bayesian inference, posterior uncertainty, interactive visualization, missing information, causal inference, treatment effects, forecasting

Figure note: A useful image would be one of the paper’s system or pipeline figures showing the variance-delta tree and interactive operations for dividing or combining subsets of unobserved quantities.

Authors: Collin Cademartori

item.title: OncoSynth: Synthetic data generation for treatment effect estimation in oncology
item.description:
Priority: 49/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 20, Paper 35, Empirical 74, Reproducibility 20, Manuscript 78

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-24; no DOI or journal reference in supplied metadata.; Abstract states restricted oncology patient-level data sharing as the motivating problem.; Method is described as a diffusion-based sequential approach modeling covariates to treatment assignment and treatment to survival.; Evaluation uses lung cancer N = 37,128 and breast cancer N = 17,046 cohorts.

TL;DR: OncoSynth proposes a diffusion-based, causally aware synthetic data framework for oncology cohorts that aims to preserve treatment-assignment and survival-outcome causal structure for more accurate treatment-effect estimation.

Problem: The paper studies synthetic patient-level data generation for oncology when real patient-level data sharing is restricted, focusing on the risk that standard synthetic data methods distort causal relationships among covariates, treatments, and outcomes and therefore bias treatment-effect estimates.

Method: It introduces OncoSynth, a generative machine learning framework using a diffusion-based sequential approach to model how covariates influence treatment assignment and how treatment affects survival.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is causal awareness in synthetic oncology cohort generation: the framework is designed not only to match patient, treatment, and outcome distributions, but also to preserve structure needed for population- and patient-level treatment-effect estimation.

Main result: From the abstract, OncoSynth is evaluated on lung cancer cohorts with N = 37,128 and breast cancer cohorts with N = 17,046, reducing population-level treatment-effect error by up to 66% and patient-level treatment-effect error by up to 58% compared with existing approaches.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify baseline methods, ablations, uncertainty intervals, robustness checks, external validation, code availability, public data availability, affiliations, DOI, or peer-review status.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causality' because the paper directly targets preservation of causal relationships for treatment-effect estimation, rather than generic distributional fidelity in synthetic data.

Tags: causality, synthetic-data, treatment-effect-estimation, oncology, diffusion-models, survival-analysis, precision-medicine

Figure note: A useful figure would show the OncoSynth pipeline: covariates feeding treatment assignment, treatment and covariates feeding survival/outcomes, and the diffusion-based synthetic cohort generation process used for treatment-effect estimation.

item.title: Causal-rCM: A Unified Teacher-Forcing and Self-Forcing Open Recipe for Autoregressive Diffusion Distillation in Streaming Video Generation and Interactive World Models
item.description:
Priority: 48/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 20, Paper 35, Empirical 75, Reproducibility 50, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation counts or h-index signals, DOI, venue, or peer-review status, code repository, public training or evaluation data details, full baseline list

Score evidence: arXiv v1 technical report published 2026-06-24.; Abstract reports extensive experiments and state-of-the-art streaming video generation in frame-wise and chunk-wise settings.; Abstract reports VBench-T2V score of 84.63 for a distilled 2-step causal Wan2.1-1.3B model.; Abstract claims 10x faster convergence for continuous-time CMs compared with discrete-time CMs.

TL;DR: Causal-rCM adapts rCM diffusion distillation to autoregressive video diffusion by combining teacher-forcing consistency-model initialization with self-forcing DMD refinement for fast streaming video generation and interactive world models.

Problem: The paper studies how to distill autoregressive video diffusion models with causal diffusion transformers so they can generate streaming or action-conditioned video with very few sampling steps while preserving quality.

Method: It proposes Causal-rCM, a unified teacher-forcing and self-forcing recipe: teacher-forcing consistency models provide an offline forward-divergence initialization, while self-forcing distribution matching distillation provides on-policy reverse-divergence refinement. The abstract also reports continuous-time teacher-forcing CMs enabled by a custom-mask FlashAttention-2 JVP kernel.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is extending rCM to autoregressive video diffusion, implementing teacher-forcing continuous-time CMs for this setting, and showing that teacher-forcing CM is an effective complement to self-forcing DMD for initialization.

Main result: The abstract claims state-of-the-art streaming video generation in frame-wise and chunk-wise settings using only synthetic training data; a distilled 2-step causal Wan2.1-1.3B model reportedly reaches a VBench-T2V score of 84.63 with 1 or 2 sampling steps, and continuous-time CMs converge 10x faster than discrete-time CMs.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide code or data links, detailed baselines, ablation tables, statistical robustness, or enough information to verify the state-of-the-art claim. It also does not clarify whether the method addresses causal inference; 'causal' appears to mean autoregressive/causal video generation.

Relevance: Relevant to the matched query 'causality' mainly through causal/autoregressive diffusion transformers and causal training for streaming world models, but it is probably less relevant if the feed is intended for causal inference or causal discovery.

Tags: autoregressive video diffusion, diffusion distillation, consistency models, distribution matching distillation, teacher forcing, self forcing, streaming video generation, interactive world models

Figure note: A useful image would be the Causal-rCM pipeline showing teacher-forcing CM initialization, self-forcing DMD refinement, and how the causal/autoregressive video model streams frames or chunks.

item.title: Inner and Outer Bounds on the Secrecy Capacity of Degraded Broadcast Channels with RMSI and Transmitter CSI
item.description:
Priority: 29/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 18, Paper 35, Empirical 20, Reproducibility 15, Manuscript 65

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations absent., Citation count and h-index absent., DOI, journal, or conference venue absent., No code or repository mentioned., No public data or reproducibility artifact mentioned., No baselines, ablations, numerical experiments, or robustness analysis described in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 published 2026-06-24.; Categories are cs.IT and eess.SP.; Abstract claims inner bounds and tight outer bounds for complementary and non-complementary RMSI scenarios.; Abstract claims a Gaussian degraded broadcast channel extension.

TL;DR: The paper characterizes secrecy capacity regions for degraded broadcast channels with two confidential messages, receiver message side information, an external eavesdropper, and non-causal transmitter CSI.

Problem: It studies secure multiuser communication over degraded broadcast channels where two legitimate receivers each need independent confidential messages while an external eavesdropper is present, and where receivers may already know some message information.

Method: The paper derives inner bounds and tight outer bounds for achievable secrecy rate regions under complementary and non-complementary receiver message side information, then extends the analysis to Gaussian degraded broadcast channels with transmitter CSI.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is a complete secrecy-capacity characterization for the considered degraded settings that jointly accounts for RMSI and non-causal transmitter CSI, a combination the abstract says prior work did not fully handle.

Main result: The abstract claims the inner and outer bounds establish the secrecy capacity region for the considered degraded channel settings, and that RMSI plus CSI can expand the secrecy capacity region, especially in the Gaussian extension.

Limitation: The abstract provides no proof details, numerical results, baselines, code, affiliations, citation signals, DOI, venue, or explicit limitations; applicability beyond degraded broadcast channels is unclear from the supplied text.

Relevance: The match to the configured query 'causality' appears indirect: the paper involves non-causal access to channel state information, but it is an information-theoretic secrecy-capacity paper rather than a causality or causal-inference paper.

Tags: information-theory, secrecy-capacity, degraded-broadcast-channel, receiver-message-side-information, channel-state-information, Gaussian-channel, cs.IT, eess.SP

item.description:

Score evidence: arXiv v1 published 2026-06-24 in cs.RO, cs.AI, and cs.HC.; Abstract formalizes coaching as a non-cooperative dynamic game.; Framework combines adaptive shared control with probabilistic models of causal influence on skill evolution.; User study N=33 on first-person-view drone racing reports significant gains over state-of-the-art AI coaching baselines.

TL;DR: The paper proposes an RL-based embodied AI coaching framework that uses adaptive shared control and causal models of coaching influence to improve human motor-skill learning in FPV drone racing.

Problem: AI copilots can improve immediate task performance, but excessive assistance may cause over-reliance and skill atrophy; the paper studies how an embodied AI agent can coach humans to develop independent motor competence.

Method: It formalizes AI coaching as a non-cooperative dynamic game between a learner optimizing task performance and a coach optimizing the learner's independent competence, then trains coaching policies with reinforcement learning using adaptive shared control and probabilistic models of the coach's causal influence on skill evolution.

Key contribution: The novelty claimed in the abstract is shifting from performance-maximizing assistance to capability-aligned coaching that strategically scaffolds and steps back, allowing productive failures while modeling causal effects on learning.

Main result: A user study with N=33 in first-person-view drone racing reports significant gains in human learning outcomes over state-of-the-art AI coaching baselines, but the abstract does not provide exact metrics or effect sizes.

Limitation: The abstract does not report quantitative effect sizes, detailed baselines, ablations, robustness checks, code availability, public data, affiliations, DOI, or peer-review status beyond arXiv v1.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' because its RL coaching framework explicitly includes probabilistic models of the coach's causal influence on human skill evolution.

Tags: AI coaching, reinforcement learning, shared control, human skill learning, causal influence modeling, human-robot interaction, drone racing

Figure note: A useful image would be the framework or pipeline figure showing the learner-coach dynamic game, adaptive shared-control loop, and causal skill-evolution model.

item.description:

TL;DR: iLLaDA is an 8B fully bidirectional masked diffusion language model trained from scratch that reports broad benchmark gains over LLaDA and competitive results against Qwen2.5 7B, but it is only weakly related to a causality keyword feed because the match appears to come from “causal attention,” not causal inference.

Problem: The paper studies whether large language models can be trained competitively without the standard autoregressive factorization and causal attention paradigm.

Method: It proposes iLLaDA, an 8B masked diffusion language model with fully bidirectional attention, trained from scratch on 12T pre-training tokens and then supervised fine-tuned on a 25B-token instruction corpus for 12 epochs. It also uses variable-length generation and confidence-based multiple-choice scoring.

Key contribution: The main novelty is scaling masked diffusion language modeling with fully bidirectional attention through both pre-training and SFT at 8B scale, positioning diffusion-style LMs as a competitive alternative to autoregressive LMs.

Main result: From the abstract: iLLaDA-Base improves over LLaDA by 21.6 points on BBH and 14.9 points on ARC-Challenge; iLLaDA-Instruct improves by 14.5 points on MATH and 16.5 points on HumanEval, and remains competitive with Qwen2.5 7B on several benchmarks.

Limitation: The abstract does not mention ablations, robustness tests, training cost, inference-latency tradeoffs, dataset contamination controls, statistical significance, or detailed comparison coverage beyond selected benchmark deltas.

Relevance: Relevance to the matched query “causality” is low: the abstract discusses causal attention as an autoregressive-LM mechanism, not causality, causal reasoning, causal discovery, or causal inference. item.title: Reflective VLA: In-Context Action Consequences Make VLAs Generalize
item.description:
Priority: 60/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 30, Paper 45, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 50, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation counts or h-index signals, DOI, journal, or conference acceptance, open-source code availability, repository quality, statistical significance or confidence intervals

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-23 in cs.CV and cs.RO.; Method conditions on observation-action-consequence triplets.; Reports results on LIBERO, SimplerEnv-Bridge, LIBERO-Plus, and LIBERO-Plus-Hard.; Claims +5.4 and +4.2 percentage-point average success-rate gains under distribution shift over a matched reactive baseline.

TL;DR: Reflective VLA augments reactive vision-language-action policies with in-context observation-action-consequence triplets so robots can infer deployment-specific action-effect mappings under embodiment and environment shift.

Problem: The paper studies why reactive VLA models generalize poorly in embodied control when hidden embodiment-specific factors such as camera geometry, calibration, or actuation bias cannot be inferred from a single current observation.

Method: It proposes Reflective VLA, which conditions each decision on past observation-action-consequence triplets plus the current observation, routes all observation modalities through a VLM under shared attention, and uses a block-causal mask for parallel multi-frame training and KV-cached real-time inference.

Key contribution: The novelty is using observed action consequences as in-context evidence of the deployment-specific action-to-effect mapping, rather than merely adding more history or relying on a reactive current-frame policy.

Main result: On LIBERO-Plus and LIBERO-Plus-Hard distribution shifts, Reflective VLA improves average success rate by 5.4 and 4.2 percentage points over a matched reactive baseline, while preserving strong in-distribution performance on standard LIBERO and SimplerEnv-Bridge.

Limitation: The abstract does not report code availability, statistical uncertainty, full baseline breadth, compute cost, real-robot validation, or failure cases; evidence is limited to the summarized benchmark results and ablation claim.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' because it explicitly models observation-action-consequence triplets and uses action consequences to infer how actions cause observed scene changes, though the abstract does not claim a formal causal inference framework.

Tags: vision-language-action, robotics, embodied AI, in-context learning, action consequences, distribution shift, causality, generalization

Figure note: A useful image would be the architecture or pipeline figure showing how observation-action-consequence triplets are encoded with the current observation and routed through the VLM/action expert with the block-causal mask.

Authors: Qing Lian, Kent Yu, Lei Zhang

item.title: A Hybrid CNN-LSTM Intrusion Detection Framework for Cybersecurity in Smart Renewable Energy Grids
item.description:
Priority: 41/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 22, Paper 25, Empirical 72, Reproducibility 38, Manuscript 52

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation counts or h-index, DOI, journal or conference venue, open-source code repository, repository quality

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-23 with no DOI or journal reference in supplied metadata.; Abstract reports CNN-LSTM architecture, seven-step preprocessing workflow, SMOTE balancing, mutual-information feature selection, and temporal sequence length T=10.; Abstract includes baseline comparisons against LSTM, CNN, Random Forest, SVM, and KNN, plus an ablation for SMOTE.; Abstract reports deployment metrics: 27,800 flows/s GPU throughput, 0.082 ms/sample CPU latency, INT8 speedup of 3.1x with 0.3% accuracy loss.

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Hybrid CNN-LSTM intrusion detection system for smart renewable energy grids, targeting FDI and DoS/DDoS attacks by combining spatial feature extraction with temporal sequence modeling.

Problem: The paper studies intrusion detection for renewable energy smart grids whose IoT, AMI, and SCADA components expand exposure to stealthy false-data injection attacks and volumetric DoS/DDoS attacks.

Method: It proposes a Hybrid CNN-LSTM IDS with CNN-based spatial feature extraction and LSTM-based temporal modeling, trained after a seven-step preprocessing pipeline: imputation, min-max normalization, one-hot encoding, SMOTE balancing, mutual-information feature selection, causal temporal sequence construction with T=10, and stratified partitioning.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is a deployable CNN-LSTM pipeline that jointly handles instantaneous volumetric anomalies and slower multi-step attacks, with class balancing, temporal sequence construction, ablation evidence, and deployment-oriented latency/throughput measurements. The core CNN-LSTM idea itself is standard; the smart-grid IDS deployment framing and preprocessing/inference package appear to be the main contribution.

Main result: From the abstract, the model reports strong benchmark performance, including 98.2% precision on NSL-KDD versus 96.4% for LSTM, 95.2% for CNN, 92.7% for Random Forest, and 90.8% for SVM; SMOTE removal reduces F1 by about 3.7 percentage points; reported inference is 27,800 flows/s on GPU and 0.082 ms/sample CPU latency, with INT8 adding 3.1x speedup at 0.3% accuracy loss.

Limitation: The abstract does not report affiliations, code, public implementation, DOI, peer review venue, full dataset list, robustness tests, cross-environment validation details, or whether the 'causal' sequence construction involves actual causal modeling rather than chronological ordering. The abstract also contains a malformed result sentence, making part of the empirical comparison unclear.

Relevance: Relevant only weakly to a causality-focused feed: the paper matches the keyword through 'causal temporal sequence construction,' but the supplied abstract describes temporal sequence modeling for IDS rather than causal inference, causal discovery, treatment effects, or counterfactual reasoning.

Tags: intrusion-detection, smart-grid-cybersecurity, cnn-lstm, time-series, class-imbalance, causality-keyword-only

Figure note: A useful image would be the framework or pipeline diagram showing preprocessing, CNN spatial feature extraction, LSTM temporal modeling, classification, and deployment path to smart-grid/IED environments.

Authors: Sajib Debnath, Remon Das

item.title: Causality-Based Parametric Control Barrier Function for Safe Multi-Vehicle Interaction
item.description:

TL;DR: The paper proposes a causality-aware extension of Parametric Control Barrier Functions for adaptive safe control in multi-vehicle interactions.

Problem: It studies safe autonomous vehicle control when neighboring vehicles have heterogeneous, partially observed controllers and influence each other during interaction, making behavior modeling and safety responses difficult.

Method: The paper extends Parametric-Control Barrier Functions to multi-robot or multi-vehicle settings by embedding causality inference for inter-vehicle influence, then uses the learned Causality-based Parametric-CBF inside an adaptive safety-critical controller for the ego vehicle.

Key contribution: The novel element is explicitly reasoning about causal inter-vehicle influence inside a learned Parametric-CBF, rather than assuming homogeneous cooperative vehicles or relying on worst-case conservative safety analysis.

Main result: The abstract reports improved task efficiency in various interaction-intensive scenarios by leveraging motion flexibility in multi-vehicle systems, but it does not provide quantitative metrics, baselines, or scenario details.

Limitation: The supplied abstract does not report quantitative results, baselines, ablations, robustness tests, code, data, or implementation details, so empirical strength and reproducibility are uncertain.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched causal inference feed because causality inference is central to the proposed safe-control framework for modeling multi-agent vehicle interactions.

item.title: Beyond Shapley: Efficient Computation of Asymmetric Shapley Values
item.description:
Priority: 43/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: high)

Quality dimensions: Author 24, Paper 42, Empirical 55, Reproducibility 32, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations not supplied., Citation counts and h-index signals not supplied., No DOI, journal, or conference venue supplied., No code repository or data availability mentioned., No specific baselines, datasets, metrics, ablations, or robustness results in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1, published 2026-06-23, category cs.AI.; Abstract explicitly studies Asymmetric Shapley Values using causal graphs.; Claims polynomial-time exact ASV computation in certain contexts where SHAP is #P-hard.; Introduces equivalence classes over topological orderings and a rooted-directed-tree algorithm.

TL;DR: The paper studies causal, model-agnostic feature attribution and proposes algorithms for exact and approximate computation of Asymmetric Shapley Values that can be more tractable than standard SHAP in some causal-graph settings.

Problem: Standard Shapley/SHAP feature attribution can be computationally hard and does not naturally incorporate causal structure; the paper focuses on efficiently computing Asymmetric Shapley Values using a causal graph.

Method: It analyzes ASV computation under causal DAG constraints, introduces equivalence classes over topological orderings to reduce computation, gives a polynomial-time algorithm for rooted directed trees, and proposes an approximation algorithm for arbitrary causal DAGs using uniform random sampling of topological orderings.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim from the abstract is that ASV can be computed exactly in polynomial time in some settings where SHAP is #P-hard, plus a topological-ordering equivalence-class framework for reducing ASV computation.

Main result: The abstract reports polynomial-time exact computation in certain contexts, polynomial-time computation in the number of equivalence classes for rooted directed trees, and experiments showing practical viability on realistic causal structures; no specific metrics, datasets, or quantitative gains are provided.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify baselines, datasets, runtime numbers, approximation error bounds, code availability, or robustness tests, so empirical strength and reproducibility are uncertain.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causality' because it uses causal graphs to improve model-agnostic explainability and studies causal constraints in feature attribution.

Tags: causality, explainable-ai, shapley-values, SHAP, feature-attribution, causal-DAGs, algorithmic-complexity

Figure note: A useful figure would show the causal DAG/topological-ordering equivalence-class framework and how exact or sampled ASV computation proceeds through the pipeline.

Authors: Ezequiel Companeetz, Santiago Cifuentes, Sergio Abriola

item.title: How Modular Is a Frontier Mixture-of-Experts? A Pre-registered Causal Test in Which Apparent Expert Modularity Mostly Dissolves
item.description:
Priority: 56/100 (Skim, confidence: high)

Quality dimensions: Author 22, Paper 45, Empirical 85, Reproducibility 65, Manuscript 85

Missing quality signals: No DOI, journal, or conference venue provided., No author affiliations provided., No citation count or h-index metadata provided., No explicit open-source code claim, only atlas and ablation data release., No detailed baselines, ablation tables, metric definitions, or robustness statistics in the abstract.

Score evidence: Tests causal expert-family ablations on Command A+ MoE with 218B total and 25B active parameters.; Pre-registers six family-to-axis hypotheses before intervention.; Compares each ablation against a size-matched random-expert null.; Evaluates under four metrics, held-out independent corpus, and bootstrap confidence intervals.

TL;DR: This paper causally tests whether experts in a frontier sparse MoE form selective functional modules and finds that robust modularity mostly disappears once corpus, metric, and statistical thresholds are controlled.

Problem: The paper studies whether sparse MoE experts in a large open-weights frontier model correspond to functional modules tied to capabilities or languages, rather than merely showing correlational routing patterns.

Method: The authors build a routing-mass atlas for Command A+, pre-register six family-to-axis hypotheses, ablate expert families at inference time, compare against size-matched random-expert nulls, and evaluate selectivity across four metrics plus an independent held-out corpus with bootstrap confidence intervals. They also use Qwen3-30B-A3B as a positive control and repeat on the unquantized BF16 model.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is a pre-registered causal modularity test for frontier MoE experts showing that apparent expert modularity is often threshold-, metric-, and corpus-dependent, rather than reliably selective.

Main result: Only one of six pre-registered expert families, the Arabic-language family, survives the conservative selective-modularity criterion; a more permissive rule yields 3/6 but is threshold-sensitive. Positive control experiments recover known modular structure in Qwen3-30B-A3B, and BF16 replication argues against a 4-bit quantization artifact.

Limitation: The abstract reports results for specific MoE models and six pre-registered expert families only; it does not provide detailed task lists, full metric definitions, effect sizes, statistical tables, code quality, or broader cross-model coverage.

Relevance: The paper is highly relevant to the matched query 'causality' because it uses causal inference-time interventions, randomized null comparisons, pre-registration, held-out validation, and bootstrap confidence intervals to distinguish causal expert function from correlational routing structure.

Tags: causality, mixture-of-experts, model interpretability, expert ablation, modularity, large language models

Figure note: A useful image would be the routing-mass atlas or the causal ablation pipeline showing expert-family selection, random-expert null comparison, metric evaluation, and selectivity decision thresholds.

TL;DR: Wan-Streamer proposes a unified Transformer for real-time full-duplex audio-visual interaction using causal streaming components and block-causal attention to jointly handle perception, reasoning, generation, timing, turn management, and multimodal synchronization.

Problem: The paper studies how to build low-latency, real-time, full-duplex audio-visual interactive systems without the latency and error accumulation of cascaded VAD, ASR, LLM, TTS, animation, and video-generation pipelines.

Method: It proposes Wan-Streamer, a single end-to-end Transformer that interleaves visual, audio, and text input/output tokens, coordinated by block-causal attention, causal encoders/decoders, and low-latency multimodal token scheduling for incremental streaming.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is a native-streaming multimodal foundation model that learns response timing, turn management, and cross-modal synchronization jointly, instead of delegating these functions to separate speech, language, avatar, and video modules.

Main result: The abstract reports streaming units as short as 160 ms at 25 fps, about 200 ms model-side response latency, and about 550 ms total interaction latency when including 350 ms bidirectional network latency.

Limitation: The supplied abstract does not report benchmark comparisons, quality metrics, user studies, ablations, datasets, model size, training details, robustness tests, or code availability, so empirical breadth and reproducibility are unclear.

Relevance: This is relevant to the matched query 'causality' because the core technical mechanism is causal stream processing, including causal encoders, causal decoders, and block-causal attention for incremental low-latency multimodal generation.

item.title: Are Text-to-Image Models Inductivist Turkeys? A Counterfactual Benchmark for Causal Reasoning
item.description:
Priority: 55/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 34, Paper 55, Empirical 62, Reproducibility 45, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations absent from supplied metadata., Citation and h-index signals absent., No DOI, journal, or conference venue listed., No quantitative result values in the abstract., No explicit model list, dataset size, baselines, ablations, or statistical robustness details in the abstract., Project page is listed, but code, data, and repository quality are not confirmed from supplied metadata.

Score evidence: Introduces CF-World as a counterfactual benchmark for T2I causal reasoning.; Defines three levels: factual, explicit counterfactual, and implicit counterfactual generation.; Evaluates open-source and closed-source T2I models with a VLM-based evaluator named CF-Eval.; Introduces PRR and RRR metrics.

TL;DR: The paper introduces CF-World, a benchmark for testing whether text-to-image models can generate images in counterfactual worlds that contradict common real-world priors.

Problem: It studies whether modern text-to-image models exhibit genuine causal or counterfactual understanding, rather than relying on learned visual-textual correlations and commonsense co-occurrence patterns.

Method: The authors propose Counterfactual-World, with scenarios organized into factual generation, explicit counterfactual generation with direct visual instructions, and implicit counterfactual generation requiring deduction from altered rules. They evaluate open-source and closed-source T2I models using a VLM-based evaluator, CF-Eval, and introduce Prior Resistance Rate and Reasoning Retention Rate.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element is a counterfactual T2I benchmark that separates ordinary factual generation from explicit and implicit counterfactual generation, aiming to measure whether models can resist entrenched real-world priors and retain reasoning when visual cues are removed.

Main result: According to the abstract, all evaluated models sharply degrade from factual to counterfactual settings, suggesting that current T2I models default to familiar commonsense priors when asked to render altered causal worlds.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide quantitative scores, model names, scenario counts, baseline details, human-evaluation validation, ablations, or robustness analysis; the reliability of the VLM-based evaluator is therefore unclear from the supplied text.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the matched query 'causal reasoning' because it probes causal and counterfactual reasoning in generative vision models, especially whether T2I systems can infer consequences under altered rules rather than reproduce statistical priors.

Tags: causal reasoning, counterfactual reasoning, text-to-image generation, benchmark, vision-language evaluation, commonsense priors

Figure note: A useful image would be the paper's benchmark or pipeline figure showing CF-World's three scenario levels and the CF-Eval evaluation flow.

Authors: Jiayi Lei, Yuandong Pu, Xingyu Han, Rongpeng Zhu, Jing Xu, Jinyao Wang, Zijian Zhou, Bin Fu, Yuewen Cao, Yihao Liu, Yongsheng Li

TL;DR: The paper applies actual causality to explain unexpected failures in autonomous cyber-physical systems, with practical algorithms evaluated on a neural-network-controlled autonomous car.

Problem: Modern autonomous CPSs with neural or black-box components cannot be fully verified before deployment, so when they violate specifications after deployment, operators need principled explanations of what caused the failure.

Method: The paper extends the actual-causality framework to the CPS failure-explanation setting, addresses theoretical gaps for deriving valid explanations in this domain, and proposes two system-agnostic explanation algorithms that trade off explanation optimality against derivation efficiency.

Key contribution: Its claimed novelty is moving actual-causality-based explanation beyond simple systems such as image classifiers into cyber-physical systems, including both theoretical guidance and practical failure-explanation algorithms.

Main result: The abstract reports that the approach is demonstrated and evaluated on a neural-network-controlled autonomous car designed for collision avoidance, but it does not provide quantitative metrics, baseline comparisons, or effect sizes.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify datasets, metrics, baselines, ablations, robustness tests, code availability, or how broadly the method generalizes beyond the autonomous-car case study.

item.title: RetiSEM: Generalising Causal Models for Fragmented Biomedical Data
item.description:
Priority: 52/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 32, Paper 35, Empirical 68, Reproducibility 70, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations not supplied., Citation counts and h-index signals not supplied., No DOI, journal, or conference reference supplied., Baseline names and quantitative results not given in abstract., No ablation, robustness, or statistical uncertainty details in abstract., Repository quality and reproducibility completeness not verifiable from supplied metadata alone.

Score evidence: Paper is arXiv v1, published 2026-06-23.; Abstract proposes a domain-constrained SEM framework named RetiSEM.; Uses biologically informed variable blocks, forbidden-edge constraints, and TE/NDE/NIE mediation decomposition.; Evaluated on ten synthetic benchmark scenarios plus a fragmented NHANES and retinal-representation real-world setting.

TL;DR: RetiSEM proposes a domain-constrained structural equation modeling framework for causal graph recovery and mediation analysis in fragmented biomedical multimodal data.

Problem: The paper studies how to learn interpretable causal models when biomedical variables from clinical, molecular, and imaging sources are incomplete or not jointly observed.

Method: It proposes RetiSEM, a structural equation modeling framework that groups variables into biologically informed blocks, applies forbidden-edge constraints, and decomposes pathway effects into total effect, natural direct effect, and natural indirect effect components.

Key contribution: The main novelty is constraining SEM-based causal recovery with biomedical domain structure to make causal graph learning and mediation analysis more feasible under fragmented multimodal data, rather than relying on unconstrained causal discovery baselines.

Main result: From the abstract, RetiSEM is evaluated on ten synthetic benchmark scenarios and one fragmented real-world NHANES-plus-retinal-representation setting; it reportedly achieves lower structural error and higher causal accuracy than unconstrained baselines on synthetic benchmarks, while real-data analysis suggests retinal variables act mostly as downstream biomarker-like indicators with smaller detectable indirect effects.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify exact metrics, effect sizes, baseline names, ablations, statistical uncertainty, robustness tests, affiliations, DOI, venue, or independent validation beyond the described synthetic and real-data settings.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the causality feed because it directly addresses causal graph recovery, structural equation modeling, mediation analysis, and causal effects in biomedical AI under missing or fragmented multimodal data.

Tags: causality, structural-equation-modeling, causal-discovery, mediation-analysis, biomedical-ai, multimodal-data, retinal-imaging, limited-resource-data

Figure note: A useful figure would show the RetiSEM pipeline: fragmented biomedical inputs, biologically informed variable blocks, forbidden-edge constrained SEM graph recovery, and TE/NDE/NIE mediation effect decomposition.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a GPT-2-style two-stage Transformer transfer-learning framework for bearing fault diagnosis under simultaneous domain heterogeneity, operating-condition shifts, and scarce labeled target data.

Problem: It studies cross-domain bearing fault diagnosis in industrial settings where heterogeneous datasets, changing operating conditions, and limited labeled target data occur together, a setting the abstract says prior methods usually handle only separately.

Method: The proposed framework uses a lightweight GPT-2-style Transformer with causal self-attention for hierarchical vibration-signal feature extraction, followed by multi-source pre-training, prototype-based knowledge modulation for target adaptation, and taxonomy-adaptive classification across heterogeneous fault categories.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is an explicit knowledge-transfer pathway where pre-trained encoder weights and fault prototype embeddings act as knowledge carriers from multi-source pre-training to target-domain adaptation, rather than relying only on implicit feature alignment.

Main result: The abstract reports validation on four real-world datasets, achieving 92.61% average accuracy with only 10% labeled target data and outperforming state-of-the-art methods by 17.24 percentage points.

Limitation: The abstract does not state whether code, data splits, ablations, statistical uncertainty, robustness tests, or detailed baseline protocols are provided; the matched query 'causality' appears to come from 'causal self-attention' rather than causal inference.

item.title: When Helpfulness Overrides Causal Caution: Context-Dependent Suppression and Recovery in LLMs
item.description:
Priority: 50/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 31, Paper 42, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 30, Manuscript 76

Missing quality signals: author affiliation, author citation or h-index signal, DOI or peer-reviewed venue, open-source code, public dataset or prompt set, rubric validation details

Score evidence: Evaluates Claude Sonnet 4.6, Claude Opus 4.7, GPT 5.5, and Gemini 3.1 Pro.; Reports 480 trials and statistical tests with p < .001 across all models.; Finds a large academic-to-advisory context drop in Causal Caution maintenance.; ArXiv v1; comment says 43 pages, 3 figures, 5 tables, SSRN Abstract ID 6965680.

TL;DR: The paper argues that frontier LLMs often know how to avoid unsupported causal claims but suppress that caution when placed in practical advisory contexts.

Problem: It studies whether LLMs refrain from causal judgment when empirical evidence is insufficient, especially when prompts shift from academic evaluation to business or policy advice.

Method: The study evaluates four LLMs across 480 trials using a rubric inspired by Pearl's Causal Hierarchy, comparing academic prompts, practical advisory prompts, and a brief causal self-correction prompt.

Key contribution: The novel claim is not simply that LLMs fail at causal reasoning, but that their expression of causal caution is context-dependent: helpful advisory framing sharply reduces caution, while a short reconsideration prompt can restore it.

Main result: Causal Caution maintenance was 91.7-100.0% in academic contexts but only 6.7-18.3% in practical advisory contexts; in recommendation/rationale prompts, only 1 of 200 responses maintained caution. A self-correction prompt restored caution to 71.4-100.0%, with reported p < .001 tests.

Limitation: The abstract does not report prompt set details, code/data availability, full rubric validation, robustness checks, or whether results generalize beyond the four named models and tested contexts.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causal reasoning' because it evaluates LLM behavior around causal judgment, distinguishing causal reasoning capability from epistemic restraint under advisory pressure.

Tags: causal reasoning, LLM evaluation, causal caution, decision support, prompt framing, AI governance

Figure note: A useful image would be the paper's framework or pipeline figure showing academic versus practical advisory prompt contexts, PCH scoring, and the self-correction causal-auditing step.

Authors: Hiroshi Okumura

Query: causal reasoning, causality

item.title: An Introduction to Causal Reinforcement Learning
item.description:
Priority: 36/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 35, Paper 45, Empirical 10, Reproducibility 15, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation count, h-index or author-impact metadata, DOI, journal or conference venue, open-source code

Score evidence: arXiv v1 published 2026-06-23 in cs.AI.; Abstract explicitly proposes structural causal models as a formalization for RL environments.; Abstract names generalized policy learning, where-to-intervene, imitation learning, and counterfactual learning as CRL task classes.; No DOI, journal reference, comment, code, data, or empirical results are present in the supplied metadata.

TL;DR: This paper frames reinforcement learning environments as structural causal models and argues for a unified causal reinforcement learning view spanning online, off-policy, causal-calculus, imitation, intervention-targeting, and counterfactual learning settings.

Problem: The paper studies the conceptual and mathematical relationship between causal inference and reinforcement learning, especially how both reason over counterfactual relations but have largely developed separately.

Method: It proposes a unifying formalization in which RL environments are decomposed into autonomous causal mechanisms modeled as structural causal models, then uses this lens to relate existing learning modes and introduce additional CRL task classes.

Main result: No empirical result is reported in the abstract; the contribution appears primarily conceptual, formal, and tutorial/framework-oriented.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the configured query because it directly concerns causal inference, causality, and causal reinforcement learning, and it may help organize how causal counterfactual reasoning connects to RL problem formulations.

item.title: Can Language Model Agents be Helpful Circuit Explainers in Mechanistic Interpretability?
item.description:
Priority: 49/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 30, Paper 45, Empirical 68, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 78

Score evidence: Introduces AgenticInterpBench with 84 semi-synthetic transformer circuits and 163 component-level annotations.; Proposes HyVE: Hypothesize, Validate, Explain.; Evaluates across four LM backbones.; Reports that validation failures include incomplete validation plans, code execution errors, and unresolved hypotheses.

TL;DR: The paper studies whether LM agents can generate useful explanations for already-localized mechanistic-interpretability circuits, introducing a benchmark and an iterative hypothesis-validation explainer.

Problem: Automatic circuit localization has improved, but explaining what the localized components do remains manual, labor-intensive, and hard to standardize.

Method: The authors introduce AgenticInterpBench, built from 84 semi-synthetic transformer circuits with 163 component-level annotations, and propose HyVE, an agentic loop that observes component behavior, generates hypotheses, causally validates them, and outputs component-level and circuit-level explanations.

Key contribution: The main novelty is framing circuit explanation as an agentic hypothesize-validate-explain workflow with causal validation, evaluated on a dedicated circuit-explanation benchmark rather than only on ad hoc interpretability case studies.

Main result: Across four LM backbones, HyVE recovers useful component- and task-level explanations, but no backbone is uniformly best; failures often occur in validation through incomplete plans, code execution errors, or unresolved hypotheses.

Limitation: The abstract reports semi-synthetic benchmark results plus one Llama-3-8B arithmetic-circuit case study, but does not mention baselines against non-agentic explainers, ablations, code release, public data, statistical robustness, or broad validation on naturally trained circuits.

Relevance: Matched query is causality: the paper is relevant because HyVE uses causal validation to test hypotheses about transformer circuit components, directly connecting mechanistic interpretability with causal explanation workflows.

Tags: mechanistic-interpretability, circuit-explanation, language-model-agents, causal-validation, benchmark, transformers

item.title: Neuro-Symbolic Drive: Rule-Grounded Faithful Reasoning for Driving VLAs
item.description:
Priority: 56/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 45, Paper 45, Empirical 65, Reproducibility 65, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation counts or h-index signals, DOI or peer-reviewed venue, named comparison baselines, ablations, statistical significance or robustness tests

Score evidence: arXiv v1 published 2026-06-22.; Abstract reports fine-tuning Qwen3.5-4B with planner-derived rule-grounded traces.; Abstract gives ADE@3s and miss-rate improvements for three-camera and eight-camera perception settings.; A GitHub code base URL is included in the abstract.

TL;DR: Neuro-Symbolic Drive fine-tunes a driving VLA on rule-grounded reasoning traces extracted from classical planners so natural-language rationales are structurally coupled to the generated trajectory.

Problem: The paper studies how to make Chain-of-Thought rationales in driving VLA models faithful to the actual motion-planning decision process, rather than post-hoc or weakly connected explanations.

Method: The authors instrument classical rule-based planners in simulation, capture internal rule-evaluation traces and executed trajectories, serialize the traces into structured reasoning supervision, and use them to fine-tune Qwen3.5-4B as a driving VLA.

Key contribution: The novel element is treating rule-based planners as executable symbolic reasoning engines and converting their internal decision traces into supervised reasoning data, making the rationale-action link structural by construction.

Main result: On a simulator-generated benchmark, detailed rule-grounded reasoning reduces ADE@3s from 0.47 to 0.26 and miss rate from 8.30% to 6.40% with three-camera perception, and ADE@3s from 0.54 to 0.26 and miss rate from 10.13% to 5.99% with eight-camera perception.

Limitation: The abstract reports only simulator-generated results and does not specify real-world validation, named baselines, ablations, statistical robustness, dataset release status, or independent tests of causal faithfulness beyond construction from planner traces.

Relevance: Relevant to the matched query 'causality' because it explicitly targets causal coupling between reasoning traces and motion generation, although it appears closer to faithful/executable supervision than formal causal inference.

Tags: driving VLA, chain-of-thought, neuro-symbolic AI, faithful reasoning, motion planning, autonomous driving, causality-adjacent

Figure note: A useful image would show the pipeline from rule-based planner instrumentation to serialized rule-grounded reasoning traces, trajectory pairing, VLA fine-tuning, and motion prediction output.

item.title: Lift4D: Harmonizing Single-View 3D Estimation for 4D Reconstruction In-the-Wild
item.description:
Priority: 43/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: high)

Quality dimensions: Author 26, Paper 45, Empirical 55, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: affiliations, citation count, h-index or author impact metadata, DOI, journal or conference venue, open-source code

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper in cs.CV, published 2026-06-22.; Abstract states the target setting is monocular video reconstruction of dynamic non-rigid objects with large deformations and occlusions.; Method includes causal latent conditioning, deformable 3D Gaussian Splatting, occlusion-aware optimization, and a view-conditioned diffusion prior.; Comment lists a webpage and demos: https://lift4d.github.io.

TL;DR: Lift4D proposes a test-time optimization framework for reconstructing dynamic non-rigid 4D objects from monocular in-the-wild video by combining temporally consistent single-view 3D priors, deformable 3D Gaussian Splatting, occlusion-aware fitting, and diffusion-based completion.

Problem: The paper studies monocular 4D reconstruction of dynamic non-rigid objects under large deformation, severe occlusion, and in-the-wild video conditions, where direct 4D prediction lacks enough training data and deform-and-refine methods stop using learned priors after initialization.

Method: Lift4D adapts a single-view 3D reconstruction model with causal latent conditioning to produce temporally consistent per-frame predictions, initializes a deformable 3D Gaussian Splatting representation, then refines it with occlusion-aware video optimization and a view-conditioned diffusion prior for unobserved regions.

Key contribution: The novelty is the test-time combination of temporally conditioned single-view 3D estimates with deformable 3DGS optimization and diffusion-prior completion, so learned priors guide both initialization and recovery of occluded or unobserved geometry rather than only the first frame or initial reconstruction.

Main result: The abstract reports that Lift4D clearly improves over prior 4D reconstruction methods, especially on challenging in-the-wild sequences with severe occlusions and non-rigid motion, but gives no quantitative metrics or dataset details.

Limitation: The supplied abstract does not provide metrics, benchmark names, ablations, runtime, robustness analysis, code availability, public data, or failure cases; the relevance to the configured query 'causality' appears mostly limited to causal latent conditioning rather than causal inference.

Relevance: Relevant if the feed uses 'causality' broadly enough to include causal temporal conditioning in generative or reconstruction pipelines; otherwise this is primarily a computer vision 4D reconstruction paper, not a causality paper in the statistical or interventionist sense.

Tags: 4D reconstruction, monocular video, non-rigid reconstruction, 3D Gaussian Splatting, test-time optimization, diffusion prior, causal latent conditioning, computer vision

Figure note: A useful image would be the Lift4D pipeline figure showing monocular video input, causal latent-conditioned single-view 3D predictions, deformable 3D Gaussian Splatting initialization/refinement, occlusion-aware sculpting, and diffusion-based completion.
