item.title: Causal Inference with Multiple Misclassified Exposures: A Control Variate-Adjusted Calibration Weighting Approach
item.description:
Priority: 50/100 (Skim, confidence: high)

Quality dimensions: Author 32, Paper 45, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 82

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index metadata, DOI, journal or conference venue, open-source code, public data availability

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper in stat.ME and stat.AP, published 2026-06-22.; Abstract states calibration weighting and control variate estimators for causal inference with multiple misclassified binary exposures and clustered observations.; Abstract claims consistency without modelling the misclassification mechanism and double robustness inherited from component estimators.; Empirical application uses 651 cystic fibrosis patients ages 6-21 and reports effect estimates with a confidence interval for the sputum-based estimate.

TL;DR: The paper proposes calibration weighting plus control-variate estimators for causal inference when multiple binary exposures are misclassified, motivated by throat-swab versus sputum culture measurements in cystic fibrosis infection studies.

Problem: It studies causal effect estimation with multiple misclassified binary exposures and clustered observations, where commonly used proxy measurements such as throat swabs have imperfect sensitivity and specificity relative to gold-standard sputum cultures.

Method: The authors develop calibration weighting estimators that treat exposure misclassification as a missing-data problem and add a control-variate adjustment that uses error-prone observations to reduce variance while preserving consistency of the gold-standard estimator.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution, as supported by the abstract, is a framework for multiple misclassified exposures that combines calibration weighting with control variates, inherits double robustness from component estimators, and characterizes a bivariate efficiency ceiling caused by the probability of jointly correct classification.

Main result: Simulations reportedly confirm consistency and double robustness under model misspecification. In a cohort of 651 cystic fibrosis patients ages 6-21, swab-based estimates attenuated the estimated effect of P. aeruginosa on percent predicted FEV1 by about 69% relative to sputum-based estimates: -2.67 versus -8.52 percentage points, with a sputum-based 95% CI of -13.40 to -3.63.

Limitation: The abstract does not report code, data availability, detailed baselines, ablations, robustness checks beyond simulations under misspecification, affiliations, DOI, peer-review venue, or external validation beyond the cystic fibrosis cohort.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the matched query 'causal inference' because it proposes estimators for causal effects under exposure measurement error, a core problem in observational causal studies.

Tags: causal inference, measurement error, misclassification, calibration weighting, control variates, double robustness, clustered data, cystic fibrosis

Figure note: A useful image would be a pipeline or causal diagram showing gold-standard sputum cultures, error-prone throat swabs, multiple binary exposures, calibration weighting, and the control-variate adjustment feeding into the causal effect estimator.

Authors: Nandini Murali, Keith Barnatchez, Jordana E. Hoppe, Brandie D. Wagner, Kayleigh P. Keller, Kevin P. Josey

TL;DR: The paper argues that LLM agents should support causal-discovery workflows without supplying causal edges, priors, constraints, or conclusions, and instantiates this position in the causal-learn+ platform.

Problem: The paper studies how to use LLM/agent systems in causal discovery without letting textual associations, prompt artifacts, or hallucinated mechanisms contaminate causal evidence that should come from data, assumptions, formal algorithms, diagnostics, and expert decisions.

Method: It proposes a workflow principle and platform, causal-learn+, where agents assist with data inspection, context retrieval, assumption explanation, method recommendation, preprocessing, expert-knowledge incorporation, formal discovery, and interpretation around the causal-learn algorithm ecosystem, while causal claims remain outside the agent's authority.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is a role separation framework for agent-assisted causal discovery: agents can coordinate and explain the workflow, but must not generate graph edges, orientations, priors, constraints, or causal conclusions as evidence.

Main result: The abstract reports a case study on Big Five personality data illustrating the agent-assisted causal-discovery pipeline, but does not provide quantitative performance metrics, baselines, or robustness results.

Limitation: Evidence appears limited to a platform description and one case study in the abstract; no baselines, ablations, statistical validation, user study, open-source repository details, or data availability are stated.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the matched query "causal discovery" because it addresses how agents and LLMs should be integrated into causal-discovery workflows while preserving formal causal-evidence standards.

item.title: Evaluation Awareness Is Not One Capability: Evidence from Open Language Models
item.description:
Priority: 55/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 34, Paper 50, Empirical 85, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 85

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index data, DOI, journal or conference venue, open-source code, public datasets

Score evidence: Eight experiments across 37 open-weight models and seven families are reported in the abstract.; Detection, behavior-shift, representation-probe, steering, and correlation results are quantified.; The abstract explicitly claims evaluation awareness dimensions are weakly coupled.; arXiv v1 only; DOI, journal reference, and comment fields are empty in supplied metadata.

TL;DR: The paper argues that language-model evaluation awareness is a multivariate safety risk, not a single capability, using experiments on open-weight models to show that detecting evaluation cues, changing safety behavior, and steerable internal representations can diverge.

Problem: Safety benchmarks often assume behavior under an evaluation harness predicts deployment behavior; the paper studies how models may detect test conditions and adapt, creating an optimistic gap between benchmark compliance and real deployment safety.

Method: The authors run eight experiments across 37 open-weight models from seven families, measuring evaluation-cue detection, safety behavior changes under framing shifts, representation-level probes, multi-layer steering interventions, and correlations among these axes.

Key contribution: The genuinely new framing is the 'benchmark illusion': evaluation awareness is decomposed into separable dimensions of detectability, behavioral manifestation, and controllability, with evidence that no single awareness score reliably proxies deployment safety.

Main result: From the abstract: 24 of 37 models exceed chance at detection, best model AUROC is 0.714 versus 0.819 for humans; hard refusals drop 5.8 percentage points under hypothetical framing; compliance can rise up to 30 percentage points; probes retain AUROC 0.98 under behavior-collapsing rewrites; and only 1 of 15 axis correlations is significant, with behavioral detection versus framing resistance at rho = -0.79, p < 0.001.

Limitation: The abstract does not report code, public data, affiliations, peer-review status, detailed baselines, ablations, or robustness checks beyond the summarized experiments; evidence is also limited to open-weight models as described.

Relevance: The matched query is 'causality'; the paper is relevant because it includes causal intervention evidence via multi-layer steering that moves downstream tasks, though the central topic is language-model safety benchmarking rather than causal inference methodology.

Tags: language-model-safety, evaluation-awareness, benchmarking, open-weight-models, representation-probing, causal-steering, harmbench

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework figure separating evaluation awareness into detectability, behavioral manifestation, and controllability, with arrows showing how these axes relate to the benchmark illusion.

TL;DR: The paper quantifies the rise of militarized language in scientific abstracts from 2010-2025 and experimentally tests whether war framing affects perceived credibility, funding willingness, policy support, and urgency.

Problem: It studies whether warfare-related vocabulary has become more common in scientific abstracts and whether such framing changes how scientific claims are received by readers.

Method: The authors analyze 21.4 million abstracts from OpenAlex and PubMed, relate militaristic-language prevalence to conflict-aligned country and annual measures, and run a within-subject war-framing experiment with 801 participants and 32,040 trials.

Key contribution: The novelty is the combination of large-scale diachronic corpus evidence with a causal framing experiment, linking the spread of militarized scientific language to measurable persuasion outcomes rather than only documenting word-frequency trends.

Main result: Militaristic terms rose 48% in OpenAlex and 32% in PubMed from 2010-2025, with acceleration after 2019; experimentally, war framing reduced credibility by -0.18 Likert units with 95% CI [-0.21, -0.14], and also reduced funding willingness and policy support while slightly increasing urgency.

Limitation: The abstract does not mention open code, preregistration, full operational definitions for militaristic terms, robustness checks, alternative lexicons, sampling details for the experiment, or whether causal conclusions generalize beyond the tested framings.

Relevance: It matches the keyword 'causality' because the paper includes a within-subject framing experiment intended to provide causal evidence about the effects of militaristic language on persuasion outcomes.

TL;DR: The paper proposes using VLMs and LLMs to build hierarchical 3D scene-graph forests with open semantic and abstract concept relationships for richer robotic scene understanding and object retrieval.

Problem: Robots need functional semantic world models for complex real-world environments, but many 3D scene graph methods rely on fixed relationship classes and rigid structures that miss broader semantic links such as causal relations and environmental context.

Method: The method identifies instance-specific concept nodes and relationships with a vision-language model, expands them with an LLM to infer more abstract concepts and relations, and assembles object nodes, concept nodes, and open semantic relationships into forests of hierarchical 3D scene graphs.

Key contribution: Its main novelty is extending hierarchical 3D scene graphs beyond pre-defined relation taxonomies by adding foundation-model-derived concept nodes and open semantic relationships, including abstract context-level links relevant to robotics tasks.

Main result: The abstract reports evaluation on uHumans2 and ScanNet for relationship accuracy and relevance, plus downstream open-vocabulary object retrieval on ScanNet and a real-world indoor Boston Dynamics Spot deployment; exact metrics and baseline comparisons are not provided in the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not state quantitative results, baselines, ablations, robustness tests, code availability, or how hallucinated VLM/LLM relationships are controlled.

Relevance: Relevant to the matched query 'causality' because it explicitly targets semantic connections neglected by standard 3D scene graphs, including causal connections, though causality appears to be one example of open relationships rather than the sole focus.

item.description:

TL;DR: CRWM proposes a causal world-model prior that constrains LLM-generated reinforcement-learning rewards, aiming to enable zero-shot automated reward design without iterative environment-feedback refinement.

Problem: The paper studies Automated Reward Design for reinforcement learning, focusing on the inefficiency and causal unreliability of LLM-based reward synthesis methods that iteratively refine semantically plausible reward hypotheses using task-specific environmental feedback.

Method: It proposes a Causal Reward World Model that offline pre-trains causal topological relationships between candidate reward components and task-targeted physical variables from multi-task interaction data, then uses coarse-to-fine pre-training plus Explicit Mechanism Decoupling and Confidence-Aware Soft Fusion to form an interpretable causal skeleton used as a prior for LLM reward generation.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is shifting ARD from correlation-driven, feedback-refined LLM reward search to a white-box causal-prior framework that constrains reward generation and supports zero-shot reward design across tasks.

Main result: The abstract reports extensive experiments on complex continuous-control benchmarks where CRWM produces executable rewards without feedback-driven refinement, reduces reward-design latency, matches or surpasses state-of-the-art performance, and generalizes to unseen tasks and diverse robotic embodiments; exact metrics, benchmarks, and baselines are not provided in the abstract.

Limitation: The supplied metadata/abstract does not provide code, datasets, benchmark names, numerical results, ablations, statistical robustness, affiliations, citation signals, DOI, or venue status, so empirical strength and reproducibility remain uncertain from the abstract alone.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causality' because the central mechanism is an explicit causal model of reward components and physical task variables, applied to automated reward design for robotics and reinforcement learning.

Tags: causality, reinforcement-learning, automated-reward-design, robotics, LLM, world-model, zero-shot-generalization

item.title: Semiparametric Efficiency Theory as Differential Calculus on a Space of Probability Distributions
item.description:

Quality dimensions: Author 49, Paper 45, Empirical 15, Reproducibility 20, Manuscript 82

Score evidence: arXiv v1, published 2026-06-22.; Single listed author: Razieh Nabi.; Categories are stat.ME and math.ST.; Abstract explicitly connects semiparametric efficiency to causal inference and missing data analysis.

TL;DR: This tutorial reframes semiparametric efficiency theory and influence-function-based inference as differential calculus on a space of probability distributions, emphasizing geometric intuition for scores, tangent spaces, nuisance tangent spaces, and efficient influence functions.

Problem: The paper addresses the opacity of standard semiparametric efficiency presentations, where influence functions and tangent-space constructions are often used technically without clear geometric intuition.

Method: It proposes a tutorial framework drawing parallels with multivariable calculus: distributional paths as curves, scores as velocity vectors, influence functions as gradients, and efficient influence functions as projected gradients.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution, as described in the abstract, is a unified geometric exposition that explains why perturbation directions are functions, why tangent spaces are model-dependent, why nuisance tangent spaces depend on the target parameter, and why efficient influence functions arise via orthogonal projection.

Main result: No empirical result is reported in the abstract; this appears to be a conceptual/tutorial paper rather than an experimental study.

Limitation: The abstract does not report formal theorems, examples, empirical validation, code, exercises, or comparison against existing tutorials, so its practical usefulness must be judged from the full manuscript.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched causal inference query because semiparametric efficiency and influence-function-based estimation underpin modern causal inference methods, including one-step estimators and targeted minimum loss estimators.

Tags: semiparametric efficiency, influence functions, causal inference, missing data, tangent spaces, tutorial, statistical theory

item.title: DeepSWIP: Quotient-WMC Counterfactuals for Neural Probabilistic Logic Programs
item.description:
Priority: 62/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 36, Paper 48, Empirical 74, Reproducibility 78, Manuscript 86

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations absent from supplied metadata., Citation count and h-index signals absent., No DOI, journal, or conference acceptance information supplied., No ablation study mentioned in the abstract., No statistical significance, confidence intervals, or robustness analysis mentioned., Repository quality and runnable reproduction instructions not assessed from supplied metadata.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-18 in cs.AI.; Code URL is included in the abstract: https://github.com/saibib/deep_SWIP.; Reports validation against DeepTwin on 12,000 MPI3D queries.; Reports a 2.14x inference speedup from avoiding Twin endogenous duplication.

TL;DR: DeepSWIP gives DeepProbLog-style neural probabilistic logic programs a single-world counterfactual semantics by materializing neural predicates, applying SWIP transformations, and computing counterfactuals via quotient weighted model counting.

Problem: The paper studies how to perform counterfactual reasoning in neurosymbolic systems such as DeepProbLog, where standard inference is associational and lacks causal semantics for interventions plus evidence.

Method: It introduces DeepSWIP, which reduces fixed-context neural predicates to ordinary ProbLog choices through neural materialization, applies Single World Intervention Programs, and computes counterfactuals with weighted model counting over one transformed program.

Key contribution: The main novelty is a single-world counterfactual semantics for DeepProbLog programs that avoids Twin-network endogenous duplication while preserving exactness under finite grounding and unique-supported-model assumptions relative to the learned materialized FCM.

Main result: The abstract reports MPI3D experiments validating the transformation against a DeepTwin construction on 12,000 queries and showing a 2.14x inference speedup; a SUMO HOV experiment suggests neural miscalibration biases plug-in estimates while a scoped randomized-policy AIPW estimator removes most first-order bias for population mean and ATE estimands.

item.title: Private Rate-Double-Robust Inference
item.description:
Priority: 44/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 47, Paper 45, Empirical 20, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: affiliations, citation_count, h_index, DOI, journal_or_conference_acceptance, open_source_code

Score evidence: Abstract explicitly addresses local privacy mechanisms that inject noise into sensitive data.; Abstract claims transfer of semiparametric properties and rate-double-robustness to the private setting.; Abstract says the target-parameter class includes causal parameters.; Metadata shows arXiv v1, published 2026-06-18, with no DOI or journal reference supplied.

TL;DR: The paper develops locally private inference theory for rate-double-robust semiparametric target parameters, including causal parameters, by designing privacy mechanisms and private nuisance estimators that preserve key large-sample properties.

Problem: It studies how to perform statistically efficient inference for rate-double-robust target parameters when individual-level sensitive data are only observed through a local privacy mechanism that injects noise.

Method: The paper considers a class of target parameters indexed linearly by an infinite-dimensional component and nonlinearly by a low-dimensional regression, then shows that suitable local privacy mechanisms transfer semiparametric properties from the sensitive-data model to the private setting. It also proposes transformations of nonparametric nuisance estimators into private estimators and develops a private method-of-moments estimator for parametric nuisance models.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim in the abstract is a reconciliation of local privacy with rate-double-robust, unbiased, semiparametrically efficient inference, including preservation of nuisance-estimator convergence properties under privatization.

Main result: No empirical result is reported in the abstract. The main result appears theoretical: rate-double-robustness and semiparametric efficiency can be transferred to the locally private setting under suitable privacy mechanisms.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify assumptions, privacy-loss parameters, concrete causal estimands, simulations, empirical benchmarks, code, or robustness checks, so the practical scope and finite-sample behavior are unclear from the supplied metadata.

Relevance: The paper matched the query "causality" because the proposed target-parameter class explicitly includes causal parameters, and it may be relevant to researchers interested in causal inference under local privacy constraints.

Tags: causal-inference, local-privacy, semiparametric-inference, double-robustness, method-of-moments, theory

Figure note: A useful figure would diagram the pipeline from sensitive data through local noise injection to private nuisance estimation and rate-double-robust target-parameter inference.

item.title: Actionable Activation Directions for Detecting and Mitigating Emergent Misalignment Across Language Model Families
item.description:
Priority: 51/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 42, Paper 45, Empirical 65, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: No affiliation metadata supplied., No citation count or h-index metadata supplied., No DOI, journal, or conference venue supplied., No open-source code or repository mentioned., No public dataset or benchmark details mentioned., No statistical uncertainty or robustness analysis described in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-18.; Abstract reports four instruction-tuned model families: Qwen2.5-1.5B, Gemma-2-2B, Llama-3.2-1B, and Ministral-3-3B.; Abstract reports 99.6% separation of aligned and misaligned activations at each model's final layer.; Abstract reports causal steering reductions in code spillover of 21-51 points.

TL;DR: The paper argues that emergent misalignment induced by fine-tuning on insecure code can be detected and partly mitigated by causal activation-space directions, but that these directions are specific within a model and unreliable as specific corrections across architectures.

Problem: It studies whether language models fine-tuned on insecure code develop a causally actionable internal misalignment direction, and whether such a direction is shared across different instruction-tuned model families.

Method: The paper compares aligned and misaligned activations across four identically fine-tuned model families, constructs difference-in-means activation directions at the final layer, tests causal steering by subtracting those directions, and evaluates cross-architecture transfer using ridge-regression maps plus specificity controls.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is a two-tier specificity result: within-model activation directions appear highly separable, causally actionable, and content-specific, while cross-model transferred directions can suppress behavior but fail specificity controls because random and orthogonal directions perform comparably.

Main result: From the abstract: the final-layer direction separates aligned from misaligned activations with 99.6% accuracy across the four model families; subtracting the direction reduces code spillover by 21-51 points; cross-architecture transfer suppresses behavior by up to 46 points but is not specific under controls.

Limitation: The abstract does not report code release, datasets, full evaluation protocol, statistical robustness, ablations beyond the stated controls, or whether results generalize beyond insecure-code fine-tuning and the four small instruction-tuned model families.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the query 'causality' because the paper explicitly tests whether activation directions are causally actionable for detecting and mitigating misalignment, rather than merely correlational probes.

Tags: causal intervention, activation steering, emergent misalignment, mechanistic interpretability, cross-architecture transfer, LLM safety

Figure note: The comment says the paper has 2 figures; a useful image would likely be a pipeline or geometry figure showing within-model activation-direction steering and cross-architecture ridge-regression transfer, but no direct image URL is supplied.

item.description:

Score evidence: Abstract targets NDE and NIE estimation in causal mediation with post-treatment confounders.; Method uses a truncated EDPM model, blocked Gibbs sampler, cluster reallocation Metropolis-Hastings, and efficient-influence-function posterior correction.; Abstract states simulations and a weight management clinical trial application were conducted.; Metadata shows arXiv v1, no DOI, no journal reference, and no comment field content.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a Bayesian nonparametric causal mediation method for estimating natural direct and indirect effects when post-treatment confounders are present, using an EDPM model plus semiparametric posterior correction.

Problem: Estimating natural direct effects (NDE) and natural indirect effects (NIE) in causal mediation analysis when post-treatment confounders complicate identification and inference.

Method: A truncated Enriched Dirichlet Process mixture model is used for flexible joint distribution modeling, with a cluster reallocation Metropolis-Hastings step inside a blocked Gibbs sampler and a one-step posterior correction based on the efficient influence function.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element, as stated in the abstract, is combining Bayesian nonparametric mediation modeling with an efficient-influence-function posterior correction intended to give reliable causal-estimand-specific posteriors and frequentist coverage for NDE/NIE.

Main result: The abstract reports simulation studies and an application to a weight management clinical trial, but does not provide quantitative performance results, baselines, or effect estimates.

Limitation: No quantitative simulation results, baseline comparisons, robustness checks, code, data availability, DOI, venue, affiliations, or limitations are provided in the supplied metadata/abstract.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causality' because it directly studies causal mediation estimands and inference under post-treatment confounding.

Tags: causal mediation, Bayesian nonparametrics, Enriched Dirichlet Process mixture, natural direct effect, natural indirect effect, post-treatment confounding, semiparametric correction, efficient influence function

Figure note: A useful image would be a pipeline or graphical-model figure showing the EDPM causal mediation model, Gibbs/MH sampling loop, and one-step efficient-influence-function posterior correction for NDE/NIE.

TL;DR: EventVLA proposes sparse, event-driven visual evidence memory for long-horizon VLA robotic manipulation, using predicted future keyframe utility to preserve task-critical observations before they disappear.

Problem: The paper studies memory failures in long-horizon robotic manipulation, especially when task-relevant visual cues become occluded or unobservable and standard VLA policies cannot retain the needed evidence.

Method: It introduces EventVLA, an end-to-end VLA framework with sparse visual evidence memory: foundational visual anchors for initial and short-term context plus a dynamic Keyframe Evidence Memory module that predicts future keyframe probabilities from latent VLA embeddings and stores selected task-critical visual events.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is a foresight-driven memory mechanism that selects observations based on predicted future causal utility, avoiding compressed bottleneck memory, high-latency dual-system designs, and unselective redundant visual buffers; it also introduces RoboTwin-MeM for diagnosing non-Markovian manipulation with interactive visual evidence.

Main result: The abstract reports extensive evaluation on 17 memory-requiring simulation tasks and 4 real-world bimanual tasks, with EventVLA achieving an average success-rate improvement of +40% over state-of-the-art memory-augmented VLAs.

Limitation: The abstract does not report code availability, dataset release status, specific baselines, ablations, statistical robustness, failure cases, compute/latency costs, or how the keyframe-probability objective is supervised or validated.

Relevance: Relevant to the matched query "causality" because the method explicitly frames memory selection around the future causal utility of current observations, though the paper appears primarily about VLA memory for robotics rather than causal inference theory.

Tags: robotics, vision-language-action, long-horizon manipulation, memory, keyframe selection, causal utility, benchmark

Figure note: A framework figure showing how foundational visual anchors, latent embeddings, keyframe probability prediction, and Keyframe Evidence Memory interact with the VLA policy would be useful, but no direct image URL is provided.

item.title: GEMS: Geometric Constraints Enable Multi-Semantic Superposition in LLMs
item.description:
Priority: 57/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 32, Paper 45, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 75, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: author affiliation not supplied, citation count or h-index not supplied, DOI, venue, or journal reference absent, statistical significance or confidence intervals not described in abstract, complete baseline set not visible from abstract, robustness and failure cases not visible from abstract

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-18.; Single listed author: Yu Deng.; Comment states 30 pages, 5 figures, 20 tables.; Comment provides code and logs URL: https://github.com/LuLu663939/gems-multi-semantic-steering.

TL;DR: GEMS proposes geometric constraints that let LLM activation steering combine multiple semantic directions at inference time without the collapse seen under naive additive steering.

Problem: The paper studies why multi-direction activation steering fails when several semantic directions are injected simultaneously into LLM hidden states without retraining.

Method: It decomposes steering collapse into distributional deviation and directional interference, then proposes GEMS: norm-preserving weighted superposition, targeted attention-pathway injection, and real-time orthogonalization of semantic directions.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is a two-source geometric explanation of multi-direction steering collapse, paired with a training-free intervention framework that maps each failure source to a specific constraint.

Main result: From the abstract: on GSM8K, injecting three concurrent non-mathematical directions preserves accuracy at 98% versus a 92% baseline, while unconstrained addition collapses to 4%; on Wikitext-2, the same injection increases perplexity by only 2.2%.

Limitation: The abstract does not report statistical uncertainty, full baseline coverage, robustness details, or external validation beyond the stated tasks and qualitative architecture transfer.

Relevance: Matched query is "causality". Relevance is indirect: the paper uses component ablations and layer-level probes to argue causal roles for its constraints, but the main topic is activation steering and representation intervention rather than causal inference as a field.

Tags: activation-steering, LLMs, representation-intervention, multi-semantic-steering, geometric-constraints, causal-ablation, cs.CL, cs.LG

Figure note: A useful image would be the paper's framework figure showing the two collapse sources and the GEMS constraints: norm-preserving superposition, attention-pathway injection, and real-time orthogonalization.

item.description:

TL;DR: The paper uses gradient-based training-data attribution plus targeted unlearning to map which regions of OLMo3-7B's pretraining corpus support social reasoning versus STEM reasoning.

Problem: Document-level training-data attribution is noisy for discovering which parts of a pretraining corpus support specific model capabilities, and prior work has focused more on factual knowledge than reasoning capabilities.

Method: The authors compute TrackStar/Bergson gradient-based attribution over a working set from the de-duplicated Dolma3 mix, aggregate influence into WebOrganizer's 24-format by 24-topic taxonomy, compare social versus STEM and reasoning versus knowledge benchmarks in a 2x2 design, and use targeted machine unlearning as partial causal validation.

Key contribution: The novel contribution is capability-level provenance analysis: aggregating training influence by corpus taxonomy bins and contrasting benchmark pairs to show that social and STEM reasoning depend on qualitatively different pretraining regions, with unlearning used to test whether high-attribution bins are causally relevant.

Main result: From the abstract, social and STEM reasoning draw on distinct corpus regions, and this separation is sharper for reasoning benchmarks than for knowledge benchmarks; unlearning high-attribution bins such as Literature for SocialIQA degrades aligned benchmarks more than within-bin random baselines.

Limitation: The abstract reports only partial causal validation and does not provide effect sizes, statistical robustness, full metric tables, ablation details, or evidence that the findings generalize beyond OLMo3-7B, Dolma3, and the selected benchmarks.

Relevance: The paper is relevant to the matched query 'causality' because it moves beyond correlational attribution by using targeted machine unlearning as a partial causal test of whether influential pretraining corpus regions support specific reasoning capabilities.

item.title: Latent Confounded Causal Discovery via Lie Bracket Geometry
item.description:
Priority: 49/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 38, Paper 45, Empirical 50, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 60

Missing quality signals: Affiliation or institution metadata not supplied., Citation count and h-index not supplied., No DOI, journal, or conference venue listed., No code repository or implementation link supplied., No public dataset or benchmark names supplied., No baseline methods, ablations, statistical uncertainty, robustness checks, or quantitative metrics visible in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper posted 2026-06-17 in cs.LG and cs.AI.; Abstract introduces two named algorithms: BRIDGE and Spectral Kan-Do Flow Matching.; Abstract targets causal discovery under latent confounding.; Abstract claims experiments show discovery of causal models with latent confounders and large DAG-search-space reductions.

TL;DR: The paper proposes Lie-bracket-geometric algorithms, BRIDGE and SKFM, for causal discovery with latent confounding by using intervention-induced flow geometry to identify visible non-integrability as evidence of hidden structure.

Problem: Causal discovery under latent confounding, especially how to reduce the combinatorial search over possible DAGs when passive observations and active interventions may imply hidden or unmodeled causal structure.

Method: The paper builds on Kan-Do-Calculus, modeling interventions and conditioning through categorical Kan-extension structure, then derives local causal vector fields from Radon-Nikodym derivatives between observational and interventional measures. BRIDGE uses Lie-bracket/Frobenius residuals as a geometric screen before downstream discovery, while SKFM learns amortized intervention fields and spectrally factors latent curvature.

Key contribution: It frames latent confounding as a failure of visible causal vector fields to close under Lie brackets, using computable Frobenius residuals as witnesses of latent obstruction; this is a geometric/categorical alternative to standard constraint-based, score-based, or purely differentiable DAG search approaches.

Main result: The abstract claims detailed experiments where BRIDGE and SKFM discover causal models with latent confounders and reduce the super-exponential DAG search space by many orders of magnitude, but it does not provide datasets, metrics, baselines, or numerical results.

Limitation: The abstract gives no concrete empirical numbers, named datasets, baseline comparisons, ablations, robustness tests, code availability, or implementation details, so the strength of the evidence cannot be assessed from the supplied metadata alone.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query "causal discovery" because it directly proposes two causal discovery algorithms for settings with latent confounders, a central difficulty in causal inference.

Tags: causal discovery, latent confounding, causal inference, Lie brackets, information geometry, category theory, Kan-Do-Calculus, interventional data

Figure note: A useful image would be a pipeline diagram showing observational/interventional measures, Radon-Nikodym ratio estimation, induced causal vector fields, Lie-bracket residual screening in BRIDGE, and spectral latent-curvature factorization in SKFM.

TL;DR: TxBench-PP introduces a deterministic benchmark for evaluating whether AI agents can make realistic small-molecule preclinical pharmacology decisions from assay workflow data, finding that current agent/model configurations perform far from reliably.

Problem: The paper studies how to evaluate AI agents for drug-discovery decision-making in realistic preclinical pharmacology settings, especially whether agents can recover conclusions from assay data rather than relying on memorized literature facts.

Method: It proposes TxBench-PP, a benchmark with 100 verifiable evaluations indexed by program stage, assay type, and task structure. Agents receive realistic workflow snapshots, inspect files in a coding environment, and return structured answers that are graded deterministically.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution, as supported by the abstract, is a focused benchmark slice for small-molecule preclinical pharmacology covering MoA/PD reasoning, compound-target engagement, causal target validation, developability and safety, and translational efficacy, with deterministic grading over realistic workflow data.

Main result: Across 16 model-harness configurations, 11 models, and 4,800 trajectories, no system reliably recovered preclinical pharmacology decisions. The strongest reported configuration, Claude Opus 4.8 / Pi, passed 59.3% of endpoint attempts, followed by GPT-5.5 / Pi at 55.3%.

item.title: The Representational Limit of Scalar Interactions: An Interventional Decomposition
item.description:
Priority: 51/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 34, Paper 45, Empirical 70, Reproducibility 45, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: No affiliation or institution metadata supplied., No citation count or h-index metadata supplied., No DOI, journal, or conference venue supplied., No open-source code or repository information supplied., No ablation details supplied in the abstract., No statistical significance or uncertainty intervals supplied.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-17 in stat.ML and cs.LG.; Abstract proves the scalar-interaction limitation on a minimal 3-way XOR structural causal model.; Introduces Stochastic Hi-Fi as post-hoc, retraining-free, and based on interventional masked inference.; Reports finite-sample Monte Carlo bounds, variance reduction from coupled diamond sampling, and finite-vocabulary convergence.

TL;DR: The paper argues that scalar pairwise interaction scores cannot distinguish uniqueness, redundancy, and synergy, and proposes Stochastic Hi-Fi, an interventional masked-inference decomposition for per-feature U/R/S profiles.

Problem: It studies the representational failure of signed pairwise feature-interaction scores: scalar interactions can either miss genuinely higher-order causal structure or project it into pairwise values that mix uniqueness, redundancy, and synergy.

Method: The proposed method, Stochastic Hi-Fi, is a post-hoc and retraining-free predictability decomposition using interventional masked inference to estimate per-feature uniqueness, redundancy, and synergy profiles, with Monte Carlo estimation, coupled diamond sampling for variance reduction, and finite-vocabulary convergence guarantees.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is a formal separation between faithful interaction indices that miss pairwise effects in a 3-way XOR SCM and projective indices that smear higher-order effects into conflated pair scalars, followed by an interventional U/R/S estimator intended to avoid that scalar bottleneck.

Main result: From the abstract, Stochastic Hi-Fi recovers structure missed by scalar baselines on tabular SCMs, reporting up to 411x larger interaction-magnitude recovery ratios; it separates redundant and synergistic heads in the GPT-2 IOI circuit; and on NIH ChestX-ray14 it matches GradCAM on Pointing Game while improving Deletion AUC.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide code availability, sample sizes, exact datasets beyond named examples, statistical uncertainty, ablations, robustness checks, runtime cost, or failure cases, so empirical strength and reproducibility remain uncertain.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the causality query through structural causal models, interventional semantics, and causal-style decomposition of feature interactions, while also connecting to interpretability of neural models.

Tags: causality, interventional inference, feature interactions, synergy, redundancy, explainable AI, structural causal models, model interpretability

item.title: Wasserstein Policy Learning for Distributional Outcomes
item.description:
Priority: 61/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 42, Paper 82, Empirical 45, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations are not supplied., Citation counts and h-index signals are not supplied., No DOI or journal reference is supplied., No code repository is mentioned., No public data or implementation details are mentioned., No empirical baselines, ablations, simulations, or real-data evaluation are mentioned in the abstract.

Score evidence: Accepted by COLT 2026 according to the metadata comment.; arXiv version is v1, published 2026-06-17.; Abstract explicitly states IPW and doubly robust estimators.; Abstract gives finite-sample regret rate O~(sqrt(N-dim(Pi)/N)).

TL;DR: The paper develops a theoretical offline policy learning framework for causal individualized treatment rules when outcomes are full probability distributions rather than scalar rewards, using Wasserstein barycenters and utility functionals.

Problem: Standard offline policy learning in causal inference usually optimizes empirical welfare based on scalar-valued potential outcomes; this paper studies the harder setting where each potential outcome is a probability measure on the real line and policy value is defined through a utility applied to the Wasserstein barycenter of induced outcome distributions.

Method: The authors propose Wasserstein policy learning for distribution-valued outcomes and analyze policy learning based on IPW and doubly robust estimators, with finite-sample regret bounds over a combinatorial policy class and an infinite-dimensional quantile domain.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is extending offline causal policy learning from scalar outcomes to distribution-valued outcomes while proving regret guarantees whose leading rate remains governed by policy-class complexity, plus a minimax lower bound showing sharpness of the leading dependence on sample size and policy-class N-dimension.

Main result: The abstract states a finite-sample regret bound with leading dependence approximately O~(sqrt(N-dim(Pi)/N)) and a matching minimax lower bound for the leading dependence on sample size N and policy-class complexity.

Limitation: The abstract does not mention experiments, simulations, real data, code, implementation details, robustness checks, or practical performance comparisons; the guarantees also appear restricted to the one-dimensional Wasserstein setting under stated regularity conditions.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched causal inference query because it studies offline policy learning, individualized treatment rules, IPW, doubly robust estimation, and regret guarantees in a causal inference setting, though the focus is theoretical and specialized to distribution-valued outcomes.

Tags: causal inference, offline policy learning, individualized treatment rules, distributional outcomes, Wasserstein barycenter, IPW, doubly robust estimation, statistical learning theory

Figure note: A useful figure would depict the proposed pipeline: observed covariates and treatments, distribution-valued potential outcomes, IPW/DR estimation, Wasserstein barycenter construction, utility evaluation, and policy-class optimization.

TL;DR: The paper proposes explicit wave-inspired, strictly causal interaction units for learning dynamical systems, arguing that structured interactions can provide expressivity without relying mainly on generic nonlinear function approximators.

Problem: Most learned models for dynamical systems use broad nonlinear function approximation, which can require high model complexity to capture structured dynamics.

Method: It introduces explicit structured dynamical units with internal state and wave-inspired interaction patterns; the units are strictly causal to avoid algebraic loops and can be stacked into layered dynamical architectures.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is a structure-first design paradigm: expressivity is intended to emerge from explicit causal interaction organization and depth, rather than primarily from strong nonlinear parameterization.

Main result: The abstract reports experiments on a nonlinear system identification task where increasing depth improves representation quality and generalization, including under readout-only fitting and limited parameter optimization.

Limitation: Evidence appears limited to a nonlinear system identification experiment from the abstract; no baselines, ablations, robustness analysis, datasets, code, or solver/runtime comparisons are specified in the supplied metadata.

Relevance: Relevant to the matched query "causality" because the architecture is explicitly described as strictly causal and designed to eliminate algebraic loops in dynamical learning, though it does not appear to address causal inference or causal discovery.

TL;DR: Balanced Twins proposes a neural matching framework for estimating ATT in staggered-treatment time series under hidden confounding by learning latent individual representations and propensity scores before constructing individual counterfactuals.

Problem: The paper studies causal effect estimation for time series when treatment adoption is staggered across individuals, pre-treatment histories are heterogeneous, and treatment assignment may be biased by unobserved factors, making aggregate trajectory comparisons ill-defined.

Method: It introduces a neural framework that jointly learns low-dimensional latent representations of individual time series and propensity scores, then uses these estimates in a flexible individual-level matching procedure to approximate counterfactual outcomes and individual treatment effects before aggregating to ATT.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is moving synthetic-control-like counterfactual construction to the individual level for staggered interventions, while avoiding classical convexity constraints and not requiring explicit temporal modeling assumptions.

Main result: The abstract says the approach is illustrated on real-world energy consumption data and semi-synthetic ICU clinical time series, but it does not report quantitative performance, baselines, effect-size accuracy, or robustness results.

Limitation: Evidence is limited from the abstract: no numerical results, baseline comparisons, ablations, uncertainty quantification, code availability, public dataset details, or formal assumptions are provided.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query because it directly addresses causal inference for time-series interventions, hidden confounding, counterfactual estimation, propensity scores, and ATT/ITE estimation.

TL;DR: LegalWorld introduces a causally connected, full life-cycle Chinese civil litigation environment and benchmark for evaluating legal agents across consultation, drafting, trial, and related stages.

Problem: The paper studies the gap between real civil litigation, where early legal actions causally constrain later outcomes, and existing legal benchmarks or simulators that evaluate isolated subtasks or reset scenarios from shared ground truth.

Method: It proposes LegalWorld, an interactive environment modeling Chinese civil litigation as a five-stage, seven-subscenario causally connected state chain grounded in 75,309 paired Chinese civil judgments, with local memory, global case memory, and a reusable Skill/Tool library. It also introduces LongJud-Bench for cross-stage legal-agent evaluation.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element is the life-cycle simulation design: disputes persist across stages so that earlier drafting and consultation decisions affect later procedural states, rather than treating each legal task as independent.

Main result: The abstract reports 18,992 ratings from 217 legal-background evaluators indicating procedurally faithful and role-consistent trajectories, plus cross-model evaluation showing capability-level divergences with no single model backbone leading across consultation, drafting, and courtroom advocacy.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide model names, quantitative benchmark scores, baseline details, ablations, robustness checks, code availability, or public data links; resources are only promised for future release.

Relevance: The paper matches the `causality` query because its central claim is a causally connected state-chain environment where earlier litigation-stage actions shape later-stage outcomes.

item.title: UniTemp: Unlocking Video Generation in Any Temporal Order via Bidirectional Distillation
item.description:

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-17 in cs.CV.; Abstract identifies forward-only temporal generation as the core limitation of existing autoregressive video diffusion methods.; Method introduces blockwise anchor latents and a bidirectional distillation framework named UniTemp.; Abstract claims competitive short- and long-video generation compared with forward-only methods, but gives no numbers.

TL;DR: UniTemp trains one autoregressive video diffusion model to generate video forward, backward, or between given frames by addressing causal-latent boundary issues with blockwise anchor latents and bidirectional distillation.

Problem: Existing autoregressive video diffusion methods mainly generate in forward temporal order, limiting workflows that need future conditioning, backward extension, or inbetween generation.

Method: The paper proposes UniTemp, a bidirectional distillation framework for any-direction autoregressive video generation, using blockwise anchor latents to supply missing past context at block boundaries when a Causal 3D VAE is used for backward generation.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is adapting causal-latent autoregressive video diffusion to arbitrary temporal generation orders within a single student model, rather than maintaining only forward temporal causality.

Main result: The abstract reports competitive short- and long-video generation versus forward-only methods while enabling bidirectional extension, inbetween generation, looping, scene transition, and visual story generation; no quantitative metrics are provided in the supplied abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not report specific datasets, metrics, ablations, robustness tests, code availability, or quantitative comparisons, so empirical strength and reproducibility are uncertain from the supplied metadata.

Relevance: The matched query is causality: the paper directly studies limitations of causal temporal latent encoding in video diffusion and proposes a mechanism to relax forward-only causal generation order.

Tags: video-generation, diffusion-models, autoregressive-modeling, causal-vae, bidirectional-generation, inbetweening

Figure note: A useful figure would show the UniTemp pipeline: Causal 3D VAE latents, blockwise anchor latents at temporal block boundaries, bidirectional distillation from teacher to student, and inference modes for backward, forward, and inbetween generation.

TL;DR: ForecastBench-Sim proposes a Freeciv-based simulated-world benchmark for evaluating AI forecasting under controllable, immediately resolvable dynamic-world scenarios, including conditional and causal questions.

Problem: The paper studies limitations of real-world forecasting benchmarks for general-purpose AI: slow outcome resolution, rarity of tail events, and difficulty scoring counterfactual or causal questions.

Method: It introduces a benchmark pipeline built from Freeciv game rollouts: models receive a structured world-state report, answer questions about hidden future states, then the simulation is continued and forecasts are scored. The setup can generate continuous or binary questions at arbitrary horizons, intervention-paired worlds, and rare or disruptive outcomes.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element is using a controllable simulated strategy-game world to create immediately resolvable forecasting tasks, including paired intervention worlds for conditional or causal forecasting, rather than relying only on naturally resolving real-world events.

Main result: The abstract reports validation slices from model evaluations and an anonymized human pilot, but does not provide quantitative performance numbers or detailed comparisons.

Limitation: The abstract does not show benchmark scale, model leaderboard results, baselines, ablations, robustness checks, code quality, or evidence that Freeciv forecasting transfers to real-world forecasting ability.

Relevance: This is relevant to the matched query `causality` because the benchmark explicitly supports paired intervention worlds for conditional or causal questions, giving a controlled testbed for causal forecasting and counterfactual reasoning.

item.title: APT: Atomic Physical Transitions for Causal Video-Language Understanding
item.description:
Priority: 52/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 36, Paper 45, Empirical 68, Reproducibility 45, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations are not supplied., Citation counts and h-index signals are not supplied., No DOI, journal, or conference reference is supplied., No code repository or data release link is supplied., Exact tuned metrics are not in the abstract., Ablations, statistical robustness, and detailed baseline comparisons are not visible in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-17 in cs.CV and cs.AI.; Defines APTs as minimal temporally localized state changes with visible cues, physical mechanisms, and before/after dynamical regimes.; Dataset covers 14 transition types and 27,303 timed instances over 1,246 trials.; Reports zero-shot VLM recall at most 14% for transition-level physics.

TL;DR: The paper introduces Atomic Physical Transitions and APT-Tune to supervise video-language models on causal, transition-level physical state changes rather than only clip-level event labels.

Problem: Clip-level video event labels such as "bounce" hide the causal physical process that makes an event valid, so current VLMs may recognize event names without understanding the underlying state transitions.

Method: The paper defines Atomic Physical Transitions as minimal temporally localized state changes linking visible cues, active physical mechanisms, and before/after dynamical regimes; builds mixed human-annotated and simulator-derived APT data; and proposes APT-Tune, a parameter-efficient LoRA tuning recipe with image-pad-aware supervision, format-conditional co-training, and mechanism-conditioned domain-to-type decoding.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is framing physical video understanding as ordered causal transition chains and using those chains as supervision for VLMs, rather than treating them as another event-label answer format.

Main result: From the abstract: current VLMs perform poorly on transition-level physics, with zero-shot recall at most 14%; direct fine-tuning improves transition detection but causes event-level forgetting; APT-Tune improves APT recall and event-level video transfer using 11M LoRA parameters on Qwen3-VL-2B. Exact post-tuning metrics are not provided in the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not report exact final scores, baselines beyond current VLMs/direct fine-tuning, ablations, robustness tests, public code, public dataset release, or evaluation breadth beyond the stated APT data.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query "causality" because the paper explicitly models videos as causal chains of physical state transitions and evaluates whether VLMs can detect transition-level causal mechanisms.

Tags: causality, video-language models, physical reasoning, computer vision, LoRA fine-tuning, event understanding, simulation data

Figure note: A useful image would be the paper's pipeline or framework figure showing how video clips are converted into ordered APT chains and how APT-Tune trains/decodes causal transition predictions.

item.title: TS-Fault: Benchmarking Time Series Forecasters Against Structural Faults
item.description:
Priority: 60/100 (Skim, confidence: high)

Quality dimensions: Author 44, Paper 45, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 70, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index information, DOI, journal, or conference acceptance, citation or influential-citation hints, statistical significance or confidence intervals, ablation studies

Score evidence: arXiv v1, published 2026-06-16.; Public code URL is included in the abstract: https://github.com/Ray-zyy/TS-Fault.; Benchmark evaluates 21 models across 6 datasets, 4 modes, and 5 difficulty levels.; Fault taxonomy uses observation-vs-mechanism and univariate-vs-multivariate axes.

TL;DR: TS-Fault is a benchmark for testing time-series forecasters under structured, parameterized fault scenarios rather than only clean held-out accuracy or generic noise corruption.

Problem: The paper studies whether standard time-series forecasting leaderboards based on average clean-data error predict deployed reliability when real systems face structured faults such as temporal events, broken cross-variable dependencies, missingness under regime change, and causal propagation through sensing pipelines.

Method: It proposes TS-Fault, a benchmark that defines four fault modes along observation-vs-mechanism and univariate-vs-multivariate axes, injects faults into prediction-critical windows using a unified importance score, and evaluates models with paired clean/corrupt protocols across controllable difficulty levels.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element, from the abstract, is treating TSF robustness as structured fault benchmarking with semantic difficulty and mechanism-level disruptions, instead of reducing robustness to i.i.d. noise sensitivity or clean-data leaderboard accuracy.

Main result: The abstract reports evaluation of 21 models across 6 datasets, 4 fault modes, and 5 difficulty levels, finding that clean-data accuracy anti-correlates with robustness, observation-level faults mostly preserve clean rankings, mechanism-level faults reshuffle rankings, and catastrophic failures occur under mechanism-level faults, especially for high-accuracy foundation models.

Limitation: The abstract does not report statistical significance, ablations, detailed dataset names, implementation details, repository quality, or whether the benchmark covers all realistic fault classes; author affiliations and citation signals are also absent from the supplied metadata.

Relevance: Relevant to the matched query 'causality' because the benchmark explicitly includes causal propagation across sensing pipelines and mechanism-level faults, though the paper appears focused on robustness benchmarking rather than causal inference methodology.

Tags: time-series forecasting, robustness benchmark, structural faults, mechanism-level faults, causal propagation, foundation models, cs.LG, stat.ML

Figure note: A useful image would be the TS-Fault pipeline or taxonomy figure showing the two fault axes, four modes, importance-score-based injection window, and paired clean/corrupt evaluation protocol.

item.title: Shrinkage priors for Bayesian Substitute Confounders
item.description:
Priority: 50/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 38, Paper 50, Empirical 58, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index data, DOI or peer-reviewed venue, open-source code, repository quality, public implementation details

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-16 in stat.ME, cs.LG, and math.ST.; Abstract explicitly targets deconfounder and substitute-confounder failure modes in multi-cause observational studies.; Claims consistency for mean potential outcomes under stated posterior concentration, contraction, overlap geometry, and latent-variable identification conditions.; Empirical evidence mentioned includes synthetic experiments and an ADNI baseline analysis.

TL;DR: The paper proposes sparse Bayesian substitute confounders for multi-cause observational causal inference, using shrinkage-prior factor assignment models to preserve shared treatment-assignment variation without over-encoding individual causes.

Problem: Multi-cause observational studies may reveal unmeasured confounding through dependence among causes, but existing substitute-confounder approaches can fit the cause distribution while producing adjustment scores that overfit the treatment vector, destroy overlap, or capture single-cause variation.

Method: A Bayesian factor assignment framework learns sparse, low-dimensional substitute confounders with shrinkage priors, then uses regression-adjusted estimators; the theory is framed through posterior concentration, factor-score contraction, and overlap-preserving assignment geometry rather than a specific prior choice.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element is the use of shrinkage-prior latent factor structure to favor multi-cause-supported factors, discourage effectively single-cause factors, and diagnose factor collapse, addressing known failure modes of flexible deconfounder-style assignment models.

Main result: Synthetic experiments illustrate effects of signal strength, outcome validity, and geometry-aware regularization; an ADNI baseline analysis finds sparse substitute scores recover much of the adjustment obtained by directly conditioning on invasive cerebrospinal-fluid biomarkers.

Limitation: The abstract does not report quantitative effect sizes, baselines, ablations, uncertainty intervals, code availability, or enough detail to judge robustness; consistency still depends on latent-variable identification assumptions.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the causality query because it studies causal adjustment for unmeasured confounding in observational multi-cause settings and directly extends deconfounder/substitute-confounder methodology.

Tags: causality, observational studies, unmeasured confounding, deconfounder, Bayesian factor models, shrinkage priors, multi-cause inference, ADNI

Figure note: A useful figure would show the Bayesian factor assignment pipeline: observed multiple causes feeding a shrinkage-prior latent factor model, sparse substitute scores, overlap/collapse diagnostics, and regression-adjusted causal estimators.

item.description:

TL;DR: The paper proposes Two-Way Synthetic Forecasting, a framework for estimating prospective causal effects in panel data by combining synthetic-control logic with multivariate time-series forecasting.

Problem: It studies how to estimate what will happen to a target unit under an intervention it has not yet experienced beyond the observed panel, rather than only reconstructing retrospective counterfactuals inside the observed period.

Method: The framework imposes low-rank temporal structure on latent time factors in panel-data latent factor models, then uses a Two-Way Synthetic Forecasting estimator that learns cross-unit relationships from pre-treatment outcomes and combines them with a time-series model learned from donor units that have experienced the intervention.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element is the prospective causal forecasting setup: it extends synthetic-controls-style counterfactual reasoning from observed-panel retrospective questions to future intervention forecasts with finite-sample error bounds, consistency, inference via orthogonalized correction, and multi-step forecasting extensions.

Main result: The abstract reports finite-sample forecasting error bounds implying pointwise consistency, asymptotic normality for an orthogonalized correction, analogous guarantees for direct and recursive multi-step forecasts, simulation evidence, and an application to NFL stadium openings during the 2020 season.

Limitation: The abstract does not report numerical empirical results, baseline comparisons, code availability, data availability, robustness checks, or detailed simulation design, so empirical strength and reproducibility cannot be assessed from the supplied metadata.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causality' because it develops methodology for causal effect estimation and inference in panel data, specifically for forecasting intervention effects prospectively.

Tags: causality, panel-data, synthetic-controls, causal-forecasting, time-series, policy-evaluation, econometrics

item.title: Concept Modulation Models: A Unified Framework for Identifiability and Extrapolation
item.description:
Priority: 42/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

TL;DR: The paper proposes Concept Modulation Models as a unified theoretical framework for identifiability and extrapolation in conditional latent variable models, including causal representation learning settings.

Problem: It studies how conditional latent variable models can reliably identify latent structure from observed attribute variation and extrapolate distributions to unseen attributes.

Method: The paper introduces Concept Modulation Models with structure A -> Lambda -> C -> X, where attributes select modulators, modulators induce latent concept laws, and concepts generate observed features. It uses attribute potentials, defined as log-density ratios between attribute-conditioned concept laws, to separate generic identifiability lifting from model-specific rigidity arguments.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is a unifying potential-based framework that connects identifiability and extrapolation guarantees across nonlinear ICA, causal representation learning, perturbation modeling, and related conditional latent variable models, rather than proving each result in a model-specific way.

Main result: From the abstract, the main result is theoretical: feature agreement on observed attributes induces a constrained latent concept transition, and extrapolation to unseen attributes holds exactly when transported attribute-potential identities extend to those attributes. No empirical result is reported in the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not mention experiments, baselines, ablations, code, public data, robustness checks, or concrete worked examples; the claims appear primarily theoretical and depend on model-specific rigidity arguments from prior work.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causal representation learning' because it explicitly targets identifiability and extrapolation in conditional latent variable models and names causal representation learning as one of the existing areas unified by the framework.

Tags: causal representation learning, identifiability, extrapolation, conditional latent variable models, generative models, nonlinear ICA, theory

Figure note: A useful figure would show the Concept Modulation Model pipeline A -> Lambda -> C -> X and how attribute potentials transport identifiability and extrapolation constraints across observed and unseen attributes.

item.title: Beyond AHI: An Interpretable Causal-Discovery-Guided Framework for Sleep Recovery in Connected Health
item.description:

Score evidence: arXiv v1 published 2026-06-16.; Accepted at the 2nd Workshop on Sensing and Computing for Smart and Connected Health, co-located with IEEE/ACM CHASE 2026.; Uses two cohorts: MESA n=1540 and MrOS n=825.; Method includes DAG learning and a two-stage screening process with physiology-based constraints and constrained LLM-assisted auditing.

TL;DR: The paper proposes an interpretable causal-discovery-guided Sleep Recovery Score from multimodal polysomnography that aligns patient-reported recovery better than AHI across two cohorts.

Problem: Objective sleep metrics such as AHI summarize PSG findings but may poorly reflect patient-reported outcomes like sleepiness, fatigue, and perceived recovery because they do not capture multidomain physiology.

Method: The authors use DAG learning on multimodal PSG features from MESA and MrOS to identify physiological drivers across respiratory burden, hypoxic burden, sleep fragmentation, sleep architecture, and autonomic regulation, then apply physiology-based constraints plus constrained LLM-assisted auditing to remove structural confounders and construct-overlapping variables before building a hierarchical Sleep Recovery Score.

Key contribution: A domain-structured, bias-aware framework that combines causal discovery with physiological constraints and LLM-assisted auditing to derive an interpretable recovery score rather than relying on a single respiratory index such as AHI.

Main result: Across MESA (n=1540) and MrOS (n=825), the resulting SRS reportedly shows up to 2.5x stronger alignment with perceived recovery than AHI.

Limitation: The abstract does not report statistical uncertainty, detailed baselines beyond AHI, ablations, external clinical validation, code availability, or robustness checks; the LLM-assisted auditing procedure is described only at a high level.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query because causal discovery and DAG learning are central to the proposed framework, applied to a concrete connected-health and clinical sleep modeling problem.

Tags: causal discovery, DAG learning, sleep medicine, polysomnography, patient-reported outcomes, connected health, interpretable ML

item.title: Apportioning Causal Responsibility of Two Risk Factors for an Adverse Outcome via Counterfactual Attribution
item.description:
Priority: 48/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 32, Paper 50, Empirical 30, Reproducibility 30, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: No DOI, journal, or conference metadata supplied., No affiliations or institution metadata supplied., No citation count, influential-citation signal, or h-index metadata supplied., No code repository or reproducibility artifact mentioned., No public dataset or implementation details mentioned., No baselines, ablations, simulations, statistical robustness, or sensitivity analyses described in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-16 in stat.ME.; Abstract states a quantitative framework for apportioning causal responsibility between two binary risk factors.; Uses latent causal types and potential outcomes under all exposure combinations.; Claims nonparametric identification under no confounding, monotonicity, and a structural balance condition.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a counterfactual, nonparametric framework for retrospectively apportioning causal responsibility between two binary risk factors for an adverse outcome.

Problem: It studies how to assign causal responsibility after an adverse outcome has already occurred, where two binary risk factors jointly contributed and the individual's full latent causal type is unobserved.

Method: The authors define average causal responsibility as expected responsibility over latent causal types defined by potential outcomes under all exposure combinations. Under no confounding and monotonicity, they identify the metric nonparametrically when a structural balance condition holds, and derive sharp bounds when it does not.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element, based on the abstract, is a formal quantitative attribution framework for splitting retrospective causal responsibility between two risk factors, rather than estimating prospective effects of causes in the usual causal-inference setting.

Main result: The abstract claims nonparametric identification under no confounding, monotonicity, and a structural balance condition, plus sharp bounds outside that balance condition. It illustrates the framework with smoking and asbestos exposures in lung cancer.

Limitation: The abstract does not report simulations, real-data validation, baselines, robustness checks, software, or how sensitive the attribution is to violations of no confounding, monotonicity, or the balance condition.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causal inference' because it develops counterfactual attribution methods, potential-outcome definitions, identification results, and bounds for causal responsibility.

Tags: causal inference, counterfactual attribution, causal responsibility, potential outcomes, nonparametric identification, sharp bounds, binary risk factors

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework diagram showing the four potential-outcome exposure combinations, latent causal types, and how expected responsibility is apportioned between the two binary risk factors under identification versus bounded cases.

Authors: Shanshan Luo, Yafang Deng, Qingyuan Zhao, Zhi Geng

Query: causal inference, causality
